Assume we sort in ascending order using a max-heap stored in an array of size \(n\).

**Heap property and basic operation.**  
A max-heap is a complete binary tree in which every node’s key is at least the keys of its children. The key subroutine is \(\text{HEAPIFY}(i)\) (“sift down”): assuming the subtrees rooted at the children of node \(i\) are already heaps, \(\text{HEAPIFY}(i)\) restores the heap property at \(i\) by swapping the element at \(i\) down along a root-to-leaf path until it is \(\ge\) its children (or reaches a leaf).

In a heap with \(m\) elements, the height is \(\lfloor \log_2 m \rfloor\), because the heap is a complete binary tree. Therefore, each call to \(\text{HEAPIFY}\) performs at most one swap per level and thus runs in
\[
T_{\text{heapify}}(m)=O(\log m).

## 1) Building the heap takes \(O(n)\)

The standard bottom-up build procedure calls \(\text{HEAPIFY}(i)\) for all internal nodes \(i=\lfloor n/2\rfloor,\dots,1\). A naive bound gives \(O(n\log n)\), but a tighter argument yields \(O(n)\).

Let the **height** \(h(i)\) of node \(i\) be the number of edges on the longest path from \(i\) to a leaf. A call \(\text{HEAPIFY}(i)\) takes \(O(h(i))\), since the element can move down at most \(h(i)\) levels.

In a complete binary tree with \(n\) nodes, the number of nodes of height exactly \(h\) is at most \(\lceil n/2^{h+1}\rceil\) (only nodes sufficiently high in the tree can have large height). Hence the total build cost is bounded by
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(h(i))
\;\le\;
\sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log n \rfloor} O\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\right)\cdot O(h)
\;=\;
O\!\left(n \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}}\right)
\;=\; O(n),
\]
since \(\sum_{h\ge 0} h/2^{h}\) converges to a constant (equal to \(2\)).

Thus, building the heap is \(O(n)\).

---

## 2) Sorting by repeated extract-max takes \(O(n\log n)\)

After building the heap, Heapsort repeats the following for \(m=n,n-1,\dots,2\):

1. Swap the root (maximum) with the last element of the heap (constant time).
2. Decrease heap size to \(m-1\) (constant time).
3. Call \(\text{HEAPIFY}(1)\) to restore the heap property on the remaining \(m-1\) elements.

For heap size \(m\), step 3 costs \(O(\log m)\). Therefore the total cost of the extraction phase is
\[
\sum_{m=2}^{n} O(\log m) \;=\; O(n\log n),
\]
because \(\log m \le \log n\) implies the sum is \(\le (n-1)\log n\), and also \(\sum_{m=2}^n \log m = \log(n!) = \Theta(n\log n)\).

---

## Conclusion

Total running time:
\[
T(n)=O(n) \;+\; O(n\log n) \;=\; O(n\log n).
\]
Hence Heapsort runs in \(O(n\log n)\) time.

Build–Heap calls Sift-Down on every internal node, starting at index ⌊n/2⌋ and moving upward.

At depth d there are at most 2ᵈ nodes, so the total work is  

  Σ_{d=0}^{h-1} 2ᵈ · O(h−d)  ≤  O( Σ_{d=0}^{h-1} 2ᵈ·(h−d) ).  

Substitute k = h−d to sum by heights:  

  = O( Σ_{k=1}^{h} 2^{h-k} · k )  
  = O( 2^{h} · Σ_{k=1}^{h} k/2^{k} )  
  ≤ O( n · 2 ) = O(n) because 2^{h} ≤ n and Σ_{k≥1} k/2^{k} = 2.

true solution:
Let \(A[1..n]\) be an array. The standard bottom-up heap construction (Build-Max-Heap) calls \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) for \(i=\lfloor n/2\rfloor,\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1,\dots,1\), where \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) assumes the subtrees rooted at \(\text{left}(i)\) and \(\text{right}(i)\) are already max-heaps and fixes the heap property at \(i\).

**Claim 1 (Heapify cost).** If node \(i\) has height \(h\) (distance in edges to the deepest leaf in its subtree), then \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) runs in \(O(h)\) time.

*Proof.* Each iteration of heapify compares \(A[i]\) with its children and possibly swaps with the larger child, then continues one level lower. The element can move down at most one level per iteration and cannot move down more than \(h\) levels. Each level uses \(O(1)\) work, so total \(O(h)\). \(\square\)

Thus the total time of Build-Heap is
\[
T(n)\;\le\;\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(\text{height}(i)).
\]
(Leaves \(i>\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) have height \(0\) and are not heapified.)

To bound this sum, count nodes by height. In a binary heap with \(n\) nodes, the number of nodes of height at least \(h\) is at most \(\left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\right\rceil\). Reason: a node of height \(\ge h\) must have a subtree containing at least \(2^{h}\) nodes below it plus itself, i.e. at least \(2^{h+1}-1\) nodes in its full binary subtree; hence there can be at most \(n/(2^{h+1})\) such nodes up to constants. More directly for heaps: the lowest level contains about half the nodes; the next level about a quarter, etc., so nodes with height \(\ge h\) occupy only the top \(n/2^{h+1}\) positions.

Let \(N_{\ge h}\) denote the number of nodes with height at least \(h\). Then
\[
N_{\ge h} \le \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil.
\]
Now use the identity that for nonnegative integers,
\[
\sum_{i} \text{height}(i) \;=\; \sum_{h\ge 1} N_{\ge h},
\]
because each node of height \(k\) contributes \(1\) to \(N_{\ge h}\) for each \(h=1,\dots,k\).

Therefore,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} N_{\ge h}
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}} + 1\right).
\]
The additive \(+1\) term is present only while \(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\ge 1\), i.e. for \(h \le \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor\), so \(\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 1 = O(\log n)\) over the relevant range. The main term is geometric:
\[
\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} = n \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 2^{-(h+1)} = n \cdot \frac{1}{4}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac12} = \frac{n}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i) = O(n).
\]
By Claim 1, the total build time satisfies
\[
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(\text{height}(i)) = O\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)\right)=O(n).
\]

Thus, building a heap from an array using the standard bottom-up procedure runs in \(O(n)\) time. \(\square\)

\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} N_{\ge h}
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}} + 1\right).
\]
The additive \(+1\) term is present only while \(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\ge 1\), i.e. for \(h \le \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor\), so \(\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 1 = O(\log n)\) over the relevant range. The main term is geometric:
\[
\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} = n \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 2^{-(h+1)} = n \cdot \frac{1}{4}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac12} = \frac{n}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i) = O(n).
\]
By Claim 1, the total build time satisfies
\[
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(\text{height}(i)) = O\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)\right)=O(n).
\]

Thus, building a heap from an array using the standard bottom-up procedure runs in \(O(n)\) time. \(\square\)

student answer:
In the bottom-up heap–build algorithm we start at the last non-leaf index ⌊n/2⌋ and “sift-down’’ each element toward the leaves.  
A node of height h (leaves have h = 0) can be moved downward at most h positions, so its individual cost is O(h).  
At height h there are at most n/2^{h+1} nodes.  
Total work ≤ Σ_{h=0}^{⌊log n⌋} (n/2^{h+1})·O(h) = O(n)·Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h}.  
The geometric/arithmetical series Σ_{h=0}^{∞} h/2^{h} converges to 2.  
Hence the whole sum is bounded by 2n, i.e. Θ(n).  
Therefore the standard bottom-up procedure builds a binary heap from any array in linear time.
true solution:
Let \(A[1..n]\) be an array. The standard bottom-up heap construction (Build-Max-Heap) calls \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) for \(i=\lfloor n/2\rfloor,\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1,\dots,1\), where \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) assumes the subtrees rooted at \(\text{left}(i)\) and \(\text{right}(i)\) are already max-heaps and fixes the heap property at \(i\).

**Claim 1 (Heapify cost).** If node \(i\) has height \(h\) (distance in edges to the deepest leaf in its subtree), then \(\text{MAX-HEAPIFY}(A,i)\) runs in \(O(h)\) time.

*Proof.* Each iteration of heapify compares \(A[i]\) with its children and possibly swaps with the larger child, then continues one level lower. The element can move down at most one level per iteration and cannot move down more than \(h\) levels. Each level uses \(O(1)\) work, so total \(O(h)\). \(\square\)

Thus the total time of Build-Heap is
\[
T(n)\;\le\;\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(\text{height}(i)).
\]
(Leaves \(i>\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) have height \(0\) and are not heapified.)

To bound this sum, count nodes by height. In a binary heap with \(n\) nodes, the number of nodes of height at least \(h\) is at most \(\left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\right\rceil\). Reason: a node of height \(\ge h\) must have a subtree containing at least \(2^{h}\) nodes below it plus itself, i.e. at least \(2^{h+1}-1\) nodes in its full binary subtree; hence there can be at most \(n/(2^{h+1})\) such nodes up to constants. More directly for heaps: the lowest level contains about half the nodes; the next level about a quarter, etc., so nodes with height \(\ge h\) occupy only the top \(n/2^{h+1}\) positions.

Let \(N_{\ge h}\) denote the number of nodes with height at least \(h\). Then
\[
N_{\ge h} \le \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil.
\]
Now use the identity that for nonnegative integers,
\[
\sum_{i} \text{height}(i) \;=\; \sum_{h\ge 1} N_{\ge h},
\]
because each node of height \(k\) contributes \(1\) to \(N_{\ge h}\) for each \(h=1,\dots,k\).

Therefore,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} N_{\ge h}
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil
\;\le\; \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}} + 1\right).
\]
The additive \(+1\) term is present only while \(\frac{n}{2^{h+1}}\ge 1\), i.e. for \(h \le \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor\), so \(\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 1 = O(\log n)\) over the relevant range. The main term is geometric:
\[
\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} = n \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 2^{-(h+1)} = n \cdot \frac{1}{4}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac12} = \frac{n}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i) = O(n).
\]
By Claim 1, the total build time satisfies
\[
T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} O(\text{height}(i)) = O\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \text{height}(i)\right)=O(n).
\]

Thus, building a heap from an array using the standard bottom-up procedure runs in \(O(n)\) time. \(\square\)

4. Assign a grade from 0 to 100 with a short justification.

Grade: <number from 0–100>  
Justification: <your explanation>
student answer:
The heap property says that in a min-heap every node’s key is ≤ its children’s keys (≥ in a max-heap) while the tree remains complete. Heaps are stored in arrays, letting the children of index i be at 2i and 2i+1 (1-based) or 2i+1 and 2i+2 (0-based). To insert, append the new key at the next open leaf, then repeatedly swap it with its parent (“sift-up”) until the property holds. To delete the root, save its value, move the last array element to the root, shrink the array, and then repeatedly swap this moved key with the smaller (or larger) child that violates the property (“sift-down”). Each sift-up or sift-down moves the key one level, so at most log n swaps occur. Thus both insertion and deletion preserve completeness and restore the heap order in O(log n) time.
true solution:
A (binary) heap is a complete binary tree (typically stored in an array) that satisfies the heap-order property.

**Heap property (heap-order).**  
For a **max-heap**, every node’s key is at least as large as the keys of its children:
\[
\forall\ \text{nodes } v,\ \text{if } c \text{ is a child of } v,\ \text{then } key(v)\ge key(c).
\]
For a **min-heap**, the inequality is reversed:
\[
key(v)\le key(c).
\]
Completeness means all levels are full except possibly the last, which is filled left-to-right; this ensures height \(\Theta(\log n)\).

When stored in a 1-indexed array \(A[1..n]\), the shape property is automatic, and parent/child indices are:
\[
parent(i)=\left\lfloor \frac{i}{2}\right\rfloor,\quad left(i)=2i,\quad right(i)=2i+1.
\]

---

## Maintaining the heap during insertion

**Goal.** Insert a new key \(x\) into a heap of size \(n\) to obtain a heap of size \(n+1\) that is still complete and satisfies heap-order.

**Step 1 (preserve completeness).**  
Place \(x\) in the next available position, i.e., at index \(n+1\) (the leftmost open spot on the last level). This preserves the complete-tree (shape) property but may violate heap-order along the path to the root.

**Step 2 (restore heap-order: sift up / percolate up).**  
While \(x\) violates heap-order with its parent, swap it with its parent. For a max-heap, while \(A[i] > A[parent(i)]\), swap \(A[i]\) and \(A[parent(i)]\) and set \(i \leftarrow parent(i)\). For a min-heap, use \(A[i] < A[parent(i)]\).

**Correctness argument (invariant).**  
After placing \(x\) at position \(i\), all nodes except possibly those on the path from \(i\) to the root satisfy heap-order. Each swap exchanges \(x\) with its parent, fixing the heap-order violation at that edge; the only possible new violation is again between \(x\) (now higher) and its new parent. Subtrees not on this path are unchanged and remain heaps. The process terminates when \(x\) reaches the root or the parent relation satisfies heap-order, at which point all edges satisfy heap-order, so the entire structure is a heap.

**Time.**  
Each swap moves \(x\) up one level. The number of levels is at most the heap height \(\lfloor \log_2(n+1)\rfloor\), so insertion runs in \(O(\log n)\).

---

## Maintaining the heap during deletion (delete-max / delete-min)

Assume **delete-max** on a max-heap (the min-heap case is symmetric).

**Goal.** Remove and return the maximum key, which is at the root \(A[1]\), while keeping the structure complete and heap-ordered.

**Step 1 (remove root, preserve completeness).**  
Save \(A[1]\) as the output. Move the last element \(A[n]\) to the root position \(A[1]\), and decrement the size to \(n-1\). This preserves completeness (the last level remains filled left-to-right) but may violate heap-order at the root.

**Step 2 (restore heap-order: sift down / percolate down / heapify).**  
Starting at the root index \(i=1\), repeatedly compare \(A[i]\) with its children. For a max-heap, let \(j\) be the index of the larger child (if any). If \(A[i] < A[j]\), swap \(A[i]\) and \(A[j]\) and set \(i \leftarrow j\); otherwise stop. (For a min-heap, choose the smaller child and swap when \(A[i] > A[j]\).)

**Correctness argument (invariant).**  
After moving the last element to the root, every node except possibly those on the path from the root down to a leaf satisfies heap-order, because only the root’s key changed. At each step, swapping the current node with its appropriate child fixes the heap-order at that node (since the larger child moves up in a max-heap), and any remaining violation can only be between the moved-down element and its new children. Subtrees not on the traversed path remain valid heaps throughout. When the process stops, the current node is at least as large as both children (or is a leaf), so heap-order holds everywhere.

**Time.**  
Each swap moves the displaced element down one level, and there are at most \(\lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor\) levels, so deletion runs in \(O(\log n)\).

---

**Summary.**  
The heap property is the heap-order condition (min-heap or max-heap) combined with completeness. Insertion maintains completeness by appending at the end and restores heap-order by sifting up. Deletion maintains completeness by moving the last element to the root and restores heap-order by sifting down. Both operations run in \(O(\log n)\) due to the heap’s height.

While \(x\) violates heap-order with its parent, swap it with its parent. For a max-heap, while \(A[i] > A[parent(i)]\), swap \(A[i]\) and \(A[parent(i)]\) and set \(i \leftarrow parent(i)\). For a min-heap, use \(A[i] < A[parent(i)]\).

**Correctness argument (invariant).**  
After placing \(x\) at position \(i\), all nodes except possibly those on the path from \(i\) to the root satisfy heap-order. Each swap exchanges \(x\) with its parent, fixing the heap-order violation at that edge; the only possible new violation is again between \(x\) (now higher) and its new parent. Subtrees not on this path are unchanged and remain heaps. The process terminates when \(x\) reaches the root or the parent relation satisfies heap-order, at which point all edges satisfy heap-order, so the entire structure is a heap.

**Time.**  
Each swap moves \(x\) up one level. The number of levels is at most the heap height \(\lfloor \log_2(n+1)\rfloor\), so insertion runs in \(O(\log n)\).

---

## Maintaining the heap during deletion (delete-max / delete-min)

Assume **delete-max** on a max-heap (the min-heap case is symmetric).

**Goal.** Remove and return the maximum key, which is at the root \(A[1]\), while keeping the structure complete and heap-ordered.

**Step 1 (remove root, preserve completeness).**  
Save \(A[1]\) as the output. Move the last element \(A[n]\) to the root position \(A[1]\), and decrement the size to \(n-1\). This preserves completeness (the last level remains filled left-to-right) but may violate heap-order at the root.

**Step 2 (restore heap-order: sift down / percolate down / heapify).**  
Starting at the root index \(i=1\), repeatedly compare \(A[i]\) with its children. For a max-heap, let \(j\) be the index of the larger child (if any). If \(A[i] < A[j]\), swap \(A[i]\) and \(A[j]\) and set \(i \leftarrow j\); otherwise stop. (For a min-heap, choose the smaller child and swap when \(A[i] > A[j]\).)

**Correctness argument (invariant).**  
After moving the last element to the root, every node except possibly those on the path from the root down to a leaf satisfies heap-order, because only the root’s key changed. At each step, swapping the current node with its appropriate child fixes the heap-order at that node (since the larger child moves up in a max-heap), and any remaining violation can only be between the moved-down element and its new children. Subtrees not on the traversed path remain valid heaps throughout. When the process stops, the current node is at least as large as both children (or is a leaf), so heap-order holds everywhere.

**Time.**  
Each swap moves the displaced element down one level, and there are at most \(\lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor\) levels, so deletion runs in \(O(\log n)\).

---

**Summary.**  
The heap property is the heap-order condition (min-heap or max-heap) combined with completeness. Insertion maintains completeness by appending at the end and restores heap-order by sifting up. Deletion maintains completeness by moving the last element to the root and restores heap-order by sifting down. Both operations run in \(O(\log n)\) due to the heap’s height.

- Provide a grade from 0 to 100, where 100 is a perfect answer and 0 is completely incorrect.

2. Check which components appear in the student answer.
3. Note any incorrect statements, missing details, or misunderstandings.
4. Assign a grade from 0 to 100 with a short justification.

Grade: <number from 0–100>  
Justification: <your explanation>
student answer:
With a doubling (and optional halving) resize strategy:  
1. Random access and overwriting are Θ(1).  
2. Pushing to the end is Θ(1) except on a full array; the k-element copy during resizing makes that single push Θ(k), but across m pushes the total extra cost forms a geometric series ≤2m, so amortized cost is Θ(1) per push.  
3. Popping from the end is Θ(1); if capacity is halved when size ≤¼ capacity, the occasional Θ(k) copy again yields Θ(1) amortized per pop.  
4. Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position requires shifting Θ(n) elements, so even amortized complexity is Θ(n).  
5. Therefore: access/update Θ(1), push_back Θ(1) amortized, pop_back Θ(1) amortized, and interior insert/delete Θ(n) amortized.
true solution:
Consider a dynamic array that stores elements in a contiguous block of memory of current capacity \(m\) and size \(n\le m\). The supported operation is

\[
\textsc{PushBack}(x): \text{ store }x\text{ at index }n\text{ and increment }n,
\]

with the rule that if \(n=m\) (array full), allocate a new array of capacity \(m' = c m\) for some constant \(c>1\) (typically \(c=2\)), copy the \(m\) existing elements into it, then insert \(x\).

We analyze amortized time using the aggregate method (and note the standard extension to other operations at the end).

---

### Aggregate analysis for \(\textsc{PushBack}\) with geometric growth

Let the cost of a normal insertion (no resize) be \(1\). When a resize happens from capacity \(m\) to \(cm\), the operation copies \(m\) elements plus does the insertion, cost \(m+1\).

Suppose we perform \(N\) calls to \(\textsc{PushBack}\) starting from an empty dynamic array with initial capacity \(1\) (any constant initial capacity changes only constants). Resizes occur when the size hits \(1, c, c^2, \dots\). Let the capacities during resizes be
\[
1, c, c^2, \ldots, c^k,
\]
where \(c^k < N \le c^{k+1}\). The total number of elements ever copied is
\[
1 + c + c^2 + \cdots + c^k \;=\; \frac{c^{k+1}-1}{c-1} \;<\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,c^k \;\le\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,N.
\]
(Using \(c^{k+1}\le cN\) and \(c^k < N\).)

The total cost over \(N\) operations equals
\[
\underbrace{N}_{\text{each insertion itself}} \;+\; \underbrace{\left(1 + c + \cdots + c^k\right)}_{\text{copies during resizes}}
\;\le\; N + \frac{c}{c-1}N \;=\; \left(1+\frac{c}{c-1}\right)N.
\]
Hence the amortized cost per \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is
\[
\frac{T(N)}{N} = O(1).
\]

For the common doubling strategy \(c=2\), the total copied elements are \(1+2+4+\cdots+2^k < 2N\), so total time \(< 3N\), giving an amortized time \(<3 = O(1)\) per append.

The worst-case time of a single \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is \(\Theta(n)\) when it triggers a resize and copies \(\Theta(n)\) elements.

---

### Other standard dynamic-array operations

1. **Random access / update** \(\textsc{Get}(i)\), \(\textsc{Set}(i,x)\):  
   Because elements are stored contiguously, address computation is constant time, so worst-case and amortized time are \(\Theta(1)\).

2. **Insert/Delete at the end** \(\textsc{PopBack}\):  
   If we do not shrink, \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is worst-case \(\Theta(1)\).  
   If we support shrinking (to save space), a standard rule is: when after a pop we have \(n < m/\alpha\) for some constant \(\alpha>1\) (e.g., \(\alpha=4\)), shrink capacity to \(m' = m/2\) (or another constant factor) and copy \(n\) elements. With appropriate hysteresis (grow at full, shrink only when sufficiently empty), the same geometric-series argument applies and \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is amortized \(O(1)\) while still having \(\Theta(n)\) worst-case when a shrink occurs.

3. **Insert/Delete at arbitrary index**:  
   Requires shifting \(\Theta(n)\) elements in the worst case, so \(\Theta(n)\) time (amortization does not remove the shifting cost).

---

### Conclusion

With geometric resizing by a constant factor \(c>1\), dynamic arrays support:
\[
\textsc{PushBack}:\ \text{worst-case } \Theta(n),\ \text{amortized } O(1);\qquad
\textsc{Get/Set}:\ \Theta(1).
\]
If shrinking is implemented with a constant-factor threshold (hysteresis), \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is also amortized \(O(1)\).

Appending an element normally costs Θ(1), and the rare Θ(n) copy during a resize is offset over the n inexpensive appends that preceded it, giving an amortized cost of Θ(1) per append.  
Similarly, if shrinking is triggered only when the array is ≤ 1/4 full, the occasional Θ(n) copy during contraction is paid for by the Θ(n) prior deletions, so each delete-at-end is also Θ(1) amortized.  
Inserting or deleting at an arbitrary interior position still requires shifting elements, so these operations remain Θ(n) even in the amortized sense.  
Thus: indexing = Θ(1), append / pop-back = Θ(1) amortized, arbitrary insert / delete = Θ(n) amortized, and the overall space used is at most 2n.
true solution:
Consider a dynamic array that stores elements in a contiguous block of memory of current capacity \(m\) and size \(n\le m\). The supported operation is

\[
\textsc{PushBack}(x): \text{ store }x\text{ at index }n\text{ and increment }n,
\]

with the rule that if \(n=m\) (array full), allocate a new array of capacity \(m' = c m\) for some constant \(c>1\) (typically \(c=2\)), copy the \(m\) existing elements into it, then insert \(x\).

We analyze amortized time using the aggregate method (and note the standard extension to other operations at the end).

---

### Aggregate analysis for \(\textsc{PushBack}\) with geometric growth

Let the cost of a normal insertion (no resize) be \(1\). When a resize happens from capacity \(m\) to \(cm\), the operation copies \(m\) elements plus does the insertion, cost \(m+1\).

Suppose we perform \(N\) calls to \(\textsc{PushBack}\) starting from an empty dynamic array with initial capacity \(1\) (any constant initial capacity changes only constants). Resizes occur when the size hits \(1, c, c^2, \dots\). Let the capacities during resizes be
\[
1, c, c^2, \ldots, c^k,
\]
where \(c^k < N \le c^{k+1}\). The total number of elements ever copied is
\[
1 + c + c^2 + \cdots + c^k \;=\; \frac{c^{k+1}-1}{c-1} \;<\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,c^k \;\le\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,N.
\]
(Using \(c^{k+1}\le cN\) and \(c^k < N\).)

The total cost over \(N\) operations equals
\[
\underbrace{N}_{\text{each insertion itself}} \;+\; \underbrace{\left(1 + c + \cdots + c^k\right)}_{\text{copies during resizes}}
\;\le\; N + \frac{c}{c-1}N \;=\; \left(1+\frac{c}{c-1}\right)N.
\]
Hence the amortized cost per \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is
\[
\frac{T(N)}{N} = O(1).
\]

For the common doubling strategy \(c=2\), the total copied elements are \(1+2+4+\cdots+2^k < 2N\), so total time \(< 3N\), giving an amortized time \(<3 = O(1)\) per append.

The worst-case time of a single \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is \(\Theta(n)\) when it triggers a resize and copies \(\Theta(n)\) elements.

---

### Other standard dynamic-array operations

1. **Random access / update** \(\textsc{Get}(i)\), \(\textsc{Set}(i,x)\):  
   Because elements are stored contiguously, address computation is constant time, so worst-case and amortized time are \(\Theta(1)\).

2. **Insert/Delete at the end** \(\textsc{PopBack}\):  
   If we do not shrink, \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is worst-case \(\Theta(1)\).  
   If we support shrinking (to save space), a standard rule is: when after a pop we have \(n < m/\alpha\) for some constant \(\alpha>1\) (e.g., \(\alpha=4\)), shrink capacity to \(m' = m/2\) (or another constant factor) and copy \(n\) elements. With appropriate hysteresis (grow at full, shrink only when sufficiently empty), the same geometric-series argument applies and \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is amortized \(O(1)\) while still having \(\Theta(n)\) worst-case when a shrink occurs.

3. **Insert/Delete at arbitrary index**:  
   Requires shifting \(\Theta(n)\) elements in the worst case, so \(\Theta(n)\) time (amortization does not remove the shifting cost).

---

### Conclusion

With geometric resizing by a constant factor \(c>1\), dynamic arrays support:
\[
\textsc{PushBack}:\ \text{worst-case } \Theta(n),\ \text{amortized } O(1);\qquad
\textsc{Get/Set}:\ \Theta(1).
\]
If shrinking is implemented with a constant-factor threshold (hysteresis), \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is also amortized \(O(1)\).

\textsc{PushBack}(x): \text{ store }x\text{ at index }n\text{ and increment }n,

with the rule that if \(n=m\) (array full), allocate a new array of capacity \(m' = c m\) for some constant \(c>1\) (typically \(c=2\)), copy the \(m\) existing elements into it, then insert \(x\).

We analyze amortized time using the aggregate method (and note the standard extension to other operations at the end).

---

### Aggregate analysis for \(\textsc{PushBack}\) with geometric growth

Let the cost of a normal insertion (no resize) be \(1\). When a resize happens from capacity \(m\) to \(cm\), the operation copies \(m\) elements plus does the insertion, cost \(m+1\).

Suppose we perform \(N\) calls to \(\textsc{PushBack}\) starting from an empty dynamic array with initial capacity \(1\) (any constant initial capacity changes only constants). Resizes occur when the size hits \(1, c, c^2, \dots\). Let the capacities during resizes be
\[
1, c, c^2, \ldots, c^k,
\]
where \(c^k < N \le c^{k+1}\). The total number of elements ever copied is
\[
1 + c + c^2 + \cdots + c^k \;=\; \frac{c^{k+1}-1}{c-1} \;<\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,c^k \;\le\; \frac{c}{c-1}\,N.
\]
(Using \(c^{k+1}\le cN\) and \(c^k < N\).)

The total cost over \(N\) operations equals
\[
\underbrace{N}_{\text{each insertion itself}} \;+\; \underbrace{\left(1 + c + \cdots + c^k\right)}_{\text{copies during resizes}}
\;\le\; N + \frac{c}{c-1}N \;=\; \left(1+\frac{c}{c-1}\right)N.
\]
Hence the amortized cost per \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is
\[
\frac{T(N)}{N} = O(1).
\]

For the common doubling strategy \(c=2\), the total copied elements are \(1+2+4+\cdots+2^k < 2N\), so total time \(< 3N\), giving an amortized time \(<3 = O(1)\) per append.

The worst-case time of a single \(\textsc{PushBack}\) is \(\Theta(n)\) when it triggers a resize and copies \(\Theta(n)\) elements.

---

### Other standard dynamic-array operations

1. **Random access / update** \(\textsc{Get}(i)\), \(\textsc{Set}(i,x)\):  
   Because elements are stored contiguously, address computation is constant time, so worst-case and amortized time are \(\Theta(1)\).

2. **Insert/Delete at the end** \(\textsc{PopBack}\):  
   If we do not shrink, \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is worst-case \(\Theta(1)\).  
   If we support shrinking (to save space), a standard rule is: when after a pop we have \(n < m/\alpha\) for some constant \(\alpha>1\) (e.g., \(\alpha=4\)), shrink capacity to \(m' = m/2\) (or another constant factor) and copy \(n\) elements. With appropriate hysteresis (grow at full, shrink only when sufficiently empty), the same geometric-series argument applies and \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is amortized \(O(1)\) while still having \(\Theta(n)\) worst-case when a shrink occurs.

3. **Insert/Delete at arbitrary index**:  
   Requires shifting \(\Theta(n)\) elements in the worst case, so \(\Theta(n)\) time (amortization does not remove the shifting cost).

---

### Conclusion

With geometric resizing by a constant factor \(c>1\), dynamic arrays support:
\[
\textsc{PushBack}:\ \text{worst-case } \Theta(n),\ \text{amortized } O(1);\qquad
\textsc{Get/Set}:\ \Theta(1).
\]
If shrinking is implemented with a constant-factor threshold (hysteresis), \(\textsc{PopBack}\) is also amortized \(O(1)\).

student answer:
Amortized analysis finds an upper bound on the average cost per operation over any long sequence, even if individual operations can be expensive.  
In the accounting (a.k.a. bank-account) method, each operation is charged an “amortized cost” that may exceed or fall below its real cost; the surplus is stored as prepaid credit on data-structure objects, and deficits are paid from previously saved credit.  
The rule is: at no time may the total stored credit become negative; this ensures that the total real cost of any sequence ≤ total amortized cost charged.  

The potential method formalizes the same idea with a numeric potential function Φ(state) ≥ 0.  
Define amortized_cost(i) = real_cost(i) + Φ(after i) − Φ(before i).  
Because ∑[Φ(after i) − Φ(before i)] telescopes to Φ(final) − Φ(initial) and Φ(initial) ≥ 0, the sum of real costs over any sequence ≤ sum of amortized costs.  
Thus, by choosing charges or a potential that keeps individual amortized_cost(i) small, we prove a small average cost per operation regardless of the worst-case pattern of requests.
true solution:
Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over any worst-case sequence of operations, without assuming a probability distribution. For a sequence of operations with actual costs \(c_1,c_2,\dots,c_m\), we define amortized costs \(\hat c_i\) so that for every \(m\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i \;\ge\; \sum_{i=1}^m c_i.
\]
Then the total actual cost of any length-\(m\) sequence is at most the total amortized cost, implying an \(O(1)\) (or other) amortized bound per operation.

Two standard ways to construct such \(\hat c_i\) are the accounting method and the potential method.

---

## 1. Accounting method (banker’s method)

Assign each operation \(i\) an amortized cost \(\hat c_i\). Interpret the difference \(\hat c_i - c_i\) as “credit” deposited (if positive) or withdrawn (if negative) from a conceptual bank that stores credits on data structure components (or globally).

Let \(B_i\) be the bank balance after operation \(i\), with \(B_0=0\). Define
\[
B_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^i (\hat c_j - c_j).
\]
A valid accounting scheme maintains the invariant
\[
B_i \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } i,
\]
meaning we never “overspend” credit. Then for any \(m\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m c_i
= \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i - B_m
\le \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i,
\]
so the amortized costs upper bound the total actual cost over every sequence.

Thus, to prove an amortized bound using accounting, it suffices to (i) choose \(\hat c_i\) for each operation type and (ii) argue that the credit invariant \(B_i\ge 0\) is preserved.

---

## 2. Potential method (physicist’s method)

Let \(D_i\) denote the state of the data structure after operation \(i\). Choose a potential function
\[
\Phi : \{\text{states}\}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}
\]
with \(\Phi(D_0)=0\). Define the amortized cost of operation \(i\) as
\[
\hat c_i \;=\; c_i + \Phi(D_i) - \Phi(D_{i-1}).
\]
Intuition: if an operation increases potential, it “stores energy/credit” for future work; if it decreases potential, it “uses up” previously stored potential to pay for current actual cost.

Summing over a sequence of \(m\) operations yields telescoping:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i
= \sum_{i=1}^m c_i + \Phi(D_m) - \Phi(D_0)
= \sum_{i=1}^m c_i + \Phi(D_m).
\]
Since \(\Phi(D_m)\ge 0\), we obtain
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m c_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i.
\]
Therefore, bounding each \(\hat c_i\) implies a bound on the total actual cost over any sequence.

---

## Relationship

The two methods are equivalent in power: the potential \(\Phi(D_i)\) corresponds to the total stored credit after operation \(i\). Accounting proves nonnegative bank balance; potential proves \(\Phi\ge 0\). Both yield a worst-case guarantee on the total cost of any operation sequence via the inequality \(\sum c_i \le \sum \hat c_i\).

Therefore, bounding each \(\hat c_i\) implies a bound on the total actual cost over any sequence.

---

## Relationship

The two methods are equivalent in power: the potential \(\Phi(D_i)\) corresponds to the total stored credit after operation \(i\). Accounting proves nonnegative bank balance; potential proves \(\Phi\ge 0\). Both yield a worst-case guarantee on the total cost of any operation sequence via the inequality \(\sum c_i \le \sum \hat c_i\).

Amortized analysis estimates the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence, guaranteeing that the total paid never exceeds the total actual work.  
In the accounting (banker’s) method, we assign an artificial “charge” to each operation: some operations are deliberately overcharged, creating stored “credits,” while others are undercharged, spending those credits.  
We prove correctness by showing (1) every operation always has enough saved credits to pay its true cost and (2) the sum of the assigned charges bounds the real total cost; the amortized cost is simply the fixed charge we chose per operation.  

The potential (physicist’s) method encodes these credits in a global numeric potential Φ(state) ≥ 0.  
Define amortized_cost(i) = actual_cost(i) + Φ(after i) − Φ(before i).  
If Φ initially is 0 and never negative, summing over n operations telescopes: Σ amortized_cost = Σ actual_cost + Φ(final) − Φ(initial) ≥ Σ actual_cost.  
Thus the average amortized_cost bounds the average real cost, yet can be easier to compute.  
Choosing Φ so that expensive operations sharply lower potential (spending stored work) and cheap ones raise it (saving work) yields tight amortized bounds, e.g., O(1) for stack operations with periodic resizing or for table doubling.
true solution:
Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation over any worst-case sequence of operations, without assuming a probability distribution. For a sequence of operations with actual costs \(c_1,c_2,\dots,c_m\), we define amortized costs \(\hat c_i\) so that for every \(m\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i \;\ge\; \sum_{i=1}^m c_i.
\]
Then the total actual cost of any length-\(m\) sequence is at most the total amortized cost, implying an \(O(1)\) (or other) amortized bound per operation.

Two standard ways to construct such \(\hat c_i\) are the accounting method and the potential method.

---

## 1. Accounting method (banker’s method)

Assign each operation \(i\) an amortized cost \(\hat c_i\). Interpret the difference \(\hat c_i - c_i\) as “credit” deposited (if positive) or withdrawn (if negative) from a conceptual bank that stores credits on data structure components (or globally).

Let \(B_i\) be the bank balance after operation \(i\), with \(B_0=0\). Define
\[
B_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^i (\hat c_j - c_j).
\]
A valid accounting scheme maintains the invariant
\[
B_i \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } i,
\]
meaning we never “overspend” credit. Then for any \(m\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m c_i
= \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i - B_m
\le \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i,
\]
so the amortized costs upper bound the total actual cost over every sequence.

Thus, to prove an amortized bound using accounting, it suffices to (i) choose \(\hat c_i\) for each operation type and (ii) argue that the credit invariant \(B_i\ge 0\) is preserved.

---

## 2. Potential method (physicist’s method)

Let \(D_i\) denote the state of the data structure after operation \(i\). Choose a potential function
\[
\Phi : \{\text{states}\}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}
\]
with \(\Phi(D_0)=0\). Define the amortized cost of operation \(i\) as
\[
\hat c_i \;=\; c_i + \Phi(D_i) - \Phi(D_{i-1}).
\]
Intuition: if an operation increases potential, it “stores energy/credit” for future work; if it decreases potential, it “uses up” previously stored potential to pay for current actual cost.

Summing over a sequence of \(m\) operations yields telescoping:
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i
= \sum_{i=1}^m c_i + \Phi(D_m) - \Phi(D_0)
= \sum_{i=1}^m c_i + \Phi(D_m).
\]
Since \(\Phi(D_m)\ge 0\), we obtain
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m c_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^m \hat c_i.
\]
Therefore, bounding each \(\hat c_i\) implies a bound on the total actual cost over any sequence.

---

## Relationship

The two methods are equivalent in power: the potential \(\Phi(D_i)\) corresponds to the total stored credit after operation \(i\). Accounting proves nonnegative bank balance; potential proves \(\Phi\ge 0\). Both yield a worst-case guarantee on the total cost of any operation sequence via the inequality \(\sum c_i \le \sum \hat c_i\).

student answer:
In a Fibonacci heap, a decrease-key operation simply lowers the key of a node and, if the heap order is violated, cuts that node (and possibly some marked ancestors) from its parent and moves each cut node to the root list. These cuts, pointer changes, and potential mark/unmark steps take O(1) actual time each. The heap postpones any tree-degree rebalancing until the next extract-min, so no consolidation work is done during decrease-key. The amortized analysis uses the potential Φ = (#trees in the root list) + 2·(#marked non-root nodes). Cutting a child increases #trees by 1 but decreases the parent’s degree, so Φ rises by at most 1; unmarking a node lowers Φ by 2, offsetting the constant cost of the cut. A cascading cut stops as soon as it reaches an unmarked parent, and every node can become marked, cut, and unmarked at most once between consecutive decreases of its key, bounding total future cuts. Hence the net potential change per decrease-key is O(1), yielding O(1) amortized time.
true solution:
A Fibonacci heap is a collection of heap-ordered trees stored in a circular doubly linked root list, with a pointer to the minimum root. Each node stores its key, degree, parent pointer, a list of children, and a boolean mark bit. The operation `decrease-key(x, k)` lowers the key of node \(x\) to \(k\le \text{key}(x)\), and if the heap order is violated (i.e., \(x\) becomes smaller than its parent), it cuts \(x\) from its parent and moves \(x\) to the root list; then it performs a *cascading cut* up the tree according to mark bits.

To prove \(O(1)\) amortized time, we use the potential method.

### Operation description (relevant parts)
Let \(y = \text{parent}(x)\).

1. Set \(\text{key}(x)\leftarrow k\).
2. If \(y\neq \text{NIL}\) and \(\text{key}(x) < \text{key}(y)\), then:
   - **Cut**: remove \(x\) from the child list of \(y\), decrement \(\deg(y)\), set \(\text{parent}(x)=\text{NIL}\), set \(\text{mark}(x)=\text{false}\), add \(x\) to the root list.
   - **Cascading-cut(y)**:
     - If \(\text{parent}(y)=\text{NIL}\): stop.
     - Else let \(z=\text{parent}(y)\). If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{false}\), set it to true and stop. If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{true}\), cut \(y\) from \(z\) (as above) and continue cascading from \(z\).
3. Update the min pointer if needed.

All pointer/list operations are \(O(1)\) worst case per cut.

### Potential function
Let
- \(t(H)\) be the number of trees (roots) in the root list of heap \(H\),
- \(m(H)\) be the number of marked nodes in \(H\).

Define the potential:
\[
\Phi(H) = t(H) + 2\,m(H).
\]
Clearly \(\Phi(H)\ge 0\) always.

The amortized cost of an operation is
\[
\hat{c} = c + \Delta \Phi,
\]
where \(c\) is the actual (worst-case) time and \(\Delta\Phi = \Phi(H_{\text{after}})-\Phi(H_{\text{before}})\).

### Amortized analysis of `decrease-key`
If no heap-order violation occurs (\(x\) is a root or \(\text{key}(x)\ge \text{key}(y)\)), the actual cost is \(O(1)\), no cuts occur, and \(\Delta\Phi=O(1)\). Hence \(\hat{c}=O(1)\).

Assume a violation occurs and cascading cuts are triggered. Suppose the operation performs:
- \(c\ge 1\) cuts total (the initial cut of \(x\) plus \(c-1\) cascading cuts), and
- during cascading cuts, there is exactly one node whose mark changes from false to true (this is the node where cascading stops), unless cascading reaches a root, in which case no node is newly marked.

We bound \(c + \Delta\Phi\).

#### Effect of one cut
Consider cutting a node \(v\) from its parent and adding it to the root list:
- The number of trees increases by 1: \(\Delta t = +1\).
- The node \(v\) becomes an unmarked root; if it was marked, it becomes unmarked: \(\Delta m \le -1\) (at worst it was marked; otherwise \(\Delta m=0\)).

Therefore for each cut,
\[
\Delta\Phi = \Delta t + 2\Delta m \le 1 + 2(-1) = -1.
\]
So each cut decreases potential by at least 1.

#### Effect of the one “stop” mark (if any)
If cascading stops at a non-root node \(u\) that was unmarked, we set \(\text{mark}(u)=\text{true}\), which increases \(m\) by 1 and thus
\[
\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}} = +2.
\]
If cascading stops at a root, there is no new mark and \(\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}}=0\).

#### Total potential change
With \(c\) cuts:
\[
\Delta\Phi \le (-1)\cdot c + 2.
\]
(The \(+2\) covers the worst case where one node becomes newly marked.)

#### Actual time
The actual time is \(O(1)\) per cut plus \(O(1)\) overhead for comparisons and pointer updates:
\[
c = \Theta(c) \quad\text{(actual cost proportional to number of cuts)}.
\]

#### Amortized time
Let the actual cost be at most \(a c + b\) for constants \(a,b\). Then:
\[
\hat{c} \;=\; (ac+b) + \Delta\Phi \;\le\; (ac+b) + (-c + 2)
\;=\; (a-1)c + (b+2).
\]
Choosing the standard constant-factor accounting where each cut costs \(O(1)\), the decrease in potential pays for the repeated cuts; the term linear in \(c\) cancels, leaving a constant bound. Formally, since \(a\) is a constant, \((a-1)c\) is \(O(c)\) but the potential drop is \(\Omega(c)\) as well; in the usual Fibonacci-heap implementation/analysis this yields a constant upper bound on \(\hat{c}\). Concretely, taking “1 unit” of actual cost per cut in the analysis, we get:
\[
\hat{c} \le c + (-c + 2) + O(1) = O(1).
\]

Hence, `decrease-key` runs in \(O(1)\) amortized time.

### Key reason the bound holds
Cascading cuts ensure that a node can lose at most one child without being cut itself: the first loss marks it, the second loss triggers its cut. The potential \(t(H)+2m(H)\) stores enough “credit” in marked nodes to pay for future cuts, and each cut reduces potential by at least 1, offsetting its constant actual cost. Therefore the total amortized cost of `decrease-key` is constant.

     - Else let \(z=\text{parent}(y)\). If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{false}\), set it to true and stop. If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{true}\), cut \(y\) from \(z\) (as above) and continue cascading from \(z\).
3. Update the min pointer if needed.

All pointer/list operations are \(O(1)\) worst case per cut.

### Potential function
Let
- \(t(H)\) be the number of trees (roots) in the root list of heap \(H\),
- \(m(H)\) be the number of marked nodes in \(H\).

Define the potential:
\[
\Phi(H) = t(H) + 2\,m(H).
\]
Clearly \(\Phi(H)\ge 0\) always.

The amortized cost of an operation is
\[
\hat{c} = c + \Delta \Phi,
\]
where \(c\) is the actual (worst-case) time and \(\Delta\Phi = \Phi(H_{\text{after}})-\Phi(H_{\text{before}})\).

### Amortized analysis of `decrease-key`
If no heap-order violation occurs (\(x\) is a root or \(\text{key}(x)\ge \text{key}(y)\)), the actual cost is \(O(1)\), no cuts occur, and \(\Delta\Phi=O(1)\). Hence \(\hat{c}=O(1)\).

Assume a violation occurs and cascading cuts are triggered. Suppose the operation performs:
- \(c\ge 1\) cuts total (the initial cut of \(x\) plus \(c-1\) cascading cuts), and
- during cascading cuts, there is exactly one node whose mark changes from false to true (this is the node where cascading stops), unless cascading reaches a root, in which case no node is newly marked.

We bound \(c + \Delta\Phi\).

#### Effect of one cut
Consider cutting a node \(v\) from its parent and adding it to the root list:
- The number of trees increases by 1: \(\Delta t = +1\).
- The node \(v\) becomes an unmarked root; if it was marked, it becomes unmarked: \(\Delta m \le -1\) (at worst it was marked; otherwise \(\Delta m=0\)).

Therefore for each cut,
\[
\Delta\Phi = \Delta t + 2\Delta m \le 1 + 2(-1) = -1.
\]
So each cut decreases potential by at least 1.

#### Effect of the one “stop” mark (if any)
If cascading stops at a non-root node \(u\) that was unmarked, we set \(\text{mark}(u)=\text{true}\), which increases \(m\) by 1 and thus
\[
\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}} = +2.
\]
If cascading stops at a root, there is no new mark and \(\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}}=0\).

#### Total potential change
With \(c\) cuts:
\[
\Delta\Phi \le (-1)\cdot c + 2.
\]
(The \(+2\) covers the worst case where one node becomes newly marked.)

#### Actual time
The actual time is \(O(1)\) per cut plus \(O(1)\) overhead for comparisons and pointer updates:
\[
c = \Theta(c) \quad\text{(actual cost proportional to number of cuts)}.
\]

#### Amortized time
Let the actual cost be at most \(a c + b\) for constants \(a,b\). Then:
\[
\hat{c} \;=\; (ac+b) + \Delta\Phi \;\le\; (ac+b) + (-c + 2)
\;=\; (a-1)c + (b+2).
\]
Choosing the standard constant-factor accounting where each cut costs \(O(1)\), the decrease in potential pays for the repeated cuts; the term linear in \(c\) cancels, leaving a constant bound. Formally, since \(a\) is a constant, \((a-1)c\) is \(O(c)\) but the potential drop is \(\Omega(c)\) as well; in the usual Fibonacci-heap implementation/analysis this yields a constant upper bound on \(\hat{c}\). Concretely, taking “1 unit” of actual cost per cut in the analysis, we get:
\[
\hat{c} \le c + (-c + 2) + O(1) = O(1).
\]

Hence, `decrease-key` runs in \(O(1)\) amortized time.

### Key reason the bound holds
Cascading cuts ensure that a node can lose at most one child without being cut itself: the first loss marks it, the second loss triggers its cut. The potential \(t(H)+2m(H)\) stores enough “credit” in marked nodes to pay for future cuts, and each cut reduces potential by at least 1, offsetting its constant actual cost. Therefore the total amortized cost of `decrease-key` is constant.

1. Set \(\text{key}(x)\leftarrow k\).
2. If \(y\neq \text{NIL}\) and \(\text{key}(x) < \text{key}(y)\), then:
   - **Cut**: remove \(x\) from the child list of \(y\), decrement \(\deg(y)\), set \(\text{parent}(x)=\text{NIL}\), set \(\text{mark}(x)=\text{false}\), add \(x\) to the root list.
   - **Cascading-cut(y)**:
     - If \(\text{parent}(y)=\text{NIL}\): stop.
     - Else let \(z=\text{parent}(y)\). If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{false}\), set it to true and stop. If \(\text{mark}(y)=\text{true}\), cut \(y\) from \(z\) (as above) and continue cascading from \(z\).
3. Update the min pointer if needed.

All pointer/list operations are \(O(1)\) worst case per cut.

### Potential function
Let
- \(t(H)\) be the number of trees (roots) in the root list of heap \(H\),
- \(m(H)\) be the number of marked nodes in \(H\).

Define the potential:
\[
\Phi(H) = t(H) + 2\,m(H).
\]
Clearly \(\Phi(H)\ge 0\) always.

The amortized cost of an operation is
\[
\hat{c} = c + \Delta \Phi,
\]
where \(c\) is the actual (worst-case) time and \(\Delta\Phi = \Phi(H_{\text{after}})-\Phi(H_{\text{before}})\).

### Amortized analysis of `decrease-key`
If no heap-order violation occurs (\(x\) is a root or \(\text{key}(x)\ge \text{key}(y)\)), the actual cost is \(O(1)\), no cuts occur, and \(\Delta\Phi=O(1)\). Hence \(\hat{c}=O(1)\).

Assume a violation occurs and cascading cuts are triggered. Suppose the operation performs:
- \(c\ge 1\) cuts total (the initial cut of \(x\) plus \(c-1\) cascading cuts), and
- during cascading cuts, there is exactly one node whose mark changes from false to true (this is the node where cascading stops), unless cascading reaches a root, in which case no node is newly marked.

We bound \(c + \Delta\Phi\).

#### Effect of one cut
Consider cutting a node \(v\) from its parent and adding it to the root list:
- The number of trees increases by 1: \(\Delta t = +1\).
- The node \(v\) becomes an unmarked root; if it was marked, it becomes unmarked: \(\Delta m \le -1\) (at worst it was marked; otherwise \(\Delta m=0\)).

Therefore for each cut,
\[
\Delta\Phi = \Delta t + 2\Delta m \le 1 + 2(-1) = -1.
\]
So each cut decreases potential by at least 1.

#### Effect of the one “stop” mark (if any)
If cascading stops at a non-root node \(u\) that was unmarked, we set \(\text{mark}(u)=\text{true}\), which increases \(m\) by 1 and thus
\[
\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}} = +2.
\]
If cascading stops at a root, there is no new mark and \(\Delta\Phi_{\text{mark}}=0\).

#### Total potential change
With \(c\) cuts:
\[
\Delta\Phi \le (-1)\cdot c + 2.
\]
(The \(+2\) covers the worst case where one node becomes newly marked.)

#### Actual time
The actual time is \(O(1)\) per cut plus \(O(1)\) overhead for comparisons and pointer updates:
\[
c = \Theta(c) \quad\text{(actual cost proportional to number of cuts)}.
\]

#### Amortized time
Let the actual cost be at most \(a c + b\) for constants \(a,b\). Then:
\[
\hat{c} \;=\; (ac+b) + \Delta\Phi \;\le\; (ac+b) + (-c + 2)
\;=\; (a-1)c + (b+2).
\]
Choosing the standard constant-factor accounting where each cut costs \(O(1)\), the decrease in potential pays for the repeated cuts; the term linear in \(c\) cancels, leaving a constant bound. Formally, since \(a\) is a constant, \((a-1)c\) is \(O(c)\) but the potential drop is \(\Omega(c)\) as well; in the usual Fibonacci-heap implementation/analysis this yields a constant upper bound on \(\hat{c}\). Concretely, taking “1 unit” of actual cost per cut in the analysis, we get:
\[
\hat{c} \le c + (-c + 2) + O(1) = O(1).
\]

Hence, `decrease-key` runs in \(O(1)\) amortized time.

### Key reason the bound holds
Cascading cuts ensure that a node can lose at most one child without being cut itself: the first loss marks it, the second loss triggers its cut. The potential \(t(H)+2m(H)\) stores enough “credit” in marked nodes to pay for future cuts, and each cut reduces potential by at least 1, offsetting its constant actual cost. Therefore the total amortized cost of `decrease-key` is constant.

student answer:
Let n = |V| and m = |E|.  
Using a binary heap, each Extract-Min and Decrease-Key is O(log n), so n Extract-Min + m Decrease-Key gives Θ((n + m) log n) ≈ Θ(m log n) for connected graphs.  
With a Fibonacci heap, Extract-Min is O(log n) amortized and Decrease-Key is O(1) amortized, yielding Θ(n log n + m).  
Hence both versions are Θ(n log n) on sparse graphs (m = O(n)), but on dense graphs the Fibonacci heap improves the m term from Θ(m log n) to Θ(m).  
Summarized: Binary heap ⇒ Θ(m log n); Fibonacci heap ⇒ Θ(n log n + m).  
Theoretical gain comes at the cost of higher constants and complexity, so binary heaps often win in practical implementations despite the asymptotic advantage of Fibonacci heaps.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|V|=n\), \(|E|=m\). Dijkstra’s algorithm maintains a priority queue \(Q\) keyed by tentative distances \(d[\cdot]\). The work is the sum of:

1. Initializations (at most \(n\) inserts into \(Q\)).
2. \(n\) iterations of extracting the minimum-distance vertex (each vertex is permanently settled once), i.e., \(n\) \(\textsc{Extract-Min}\) operations.
3. For each edge \((u,v)\in E\), one relaxation attempt; if relaxation improves \(d[v]\), one \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) on \(v\). Thus there are at most \(m\) \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) operations (one per successful relaxation, and successful relaxations are \(\le m\)).

We assume all edge weights are nonnegative so that Dijkstra’s correctness conditions hold; the complexity bounds below depend only on the number of PQ operations.

---

## Using a binary heap

Binary heap operation costs (worst-case):
\[
\textsc{Insert} = O(\log n),\quad \textsc{Extract-Min} = O(\log n),\quad \textsc{Decrease-Key} = O(\log n).
\]

Total time:
- Inserts: \(O(n\log n)\).
- Extract-min: \(n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n\log n)\).
- Decrease-key: at most \(m\) times, each \(O(\log n)\), giving \(O(m\log n)\).

Hence,
\[
T_{\text{binary}}(n,m) = O(n\log n + m\log n) = O((m+n)\log n) = O(m\log n)
\]
(since typically \(m\ge n-1\); otherwise keep \(O((m+n)\log n)\) as the precise bound).

---

## Using a Fibonacci heap

Fibonacci heap operation costs (amortized):
\[
\textsc{Insert} = O(1),\quad \textsc{Decrease-Key} = O(1),\quad \textsc{Extract-Min} = O(\log n).
\]

Total amortized time:
- Inserts: \(O(n)\).
- Extract-min: \(n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n\log n)\).
- Decrease-key: at most \(m\) times, each \(O(1)\), giving \(O(m)\).

Hence,
\[
T_{\text{Fibo}}(n,m) = O(n + n\log n + m) = O(m + n\log n).
\]

---

## Comparison

Binary heap: \(O((m+n)\log n)\) (often written \(O(m\log n)\)).

Fibonacci heap: \(O(m + n\log n)\) amortized.

Thus, Fibonacci heaps asymptotically improve Dijkstra’s running time by reducing the total cost of \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) operations from \(O(m\log n)\) to \(O(m)\), leaving the \(n\) extract-min operations as the dominant \(O(n\log n)\) term.

Therefore a Fibonacci heap improves Dijkstra’s running time from Θ(m log n) to Θ(n log n + m), a clear asymptotic gain when the graph is sparse (m » n).  
For dense graphs where m ≈ n², both approaches are Θ(n² log n), and in practice the simpler binary heap often outperforms due to lower constants.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) with \(|V|=n\), \(|E|=m\). Dijkstra’s algorithm maintains a priority queue \(Q\) keyed by tentative distances \(d[\cdot]\). The work is the sum of:

1. Initializations (at most \(n\) inserts into \(Q\)).
2. \(n\) iterations of extracting the minimum-distance vertex (each vertex is permanently settled once), i.e., \(n\) \(\textsc{Extract-Min}\) operations.
3. For each edge \((u,v)\in E\), one relaxation attempt; if relaxation improves \(d[v]\), one \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) on \(v\). Thus there are at most \(m\) \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) operations (one per successful relaxation, and successful relaxations are \(\le m\)).

We assume all edge weights are nonnegative so that Dijkstra’s correctness conditions hold; the complexity bounds below depend only on the number of PQ operations.

---

## Using a binary heap

Binary heap operation costs (worst-case):
\[
\textsc{Insert} = O(\log n),\quad \textsc{Extract-Min} = O(\log n),\quad \textsc{Decrease-Key} = O(\log n).
\]

Total time:
- Inserts: \(O(n\log n)\).
- Extract-min: \(n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n\log n)\).
- Decrease-key: at most \(m\) times, each \(O(\log n)\), giving \(O(m\log n)\).

Hence,
\[
T_{\text{binary}}(n,m) = O(n\log n + m\log n) = O((m+n)\log n) = O(m\log n)
\]
(since typically \(m\ge n-1\); otherwise keep \(O((m+n)\log n)\) as the precise bound).

---

## Using a Fibonacci heap

Fibonacci heap operation costs (amortized):
\[
\textsc{Insert} = O(1),\quad \textsc{Decrease-Key} = O(1),\quad \textsc{Extract-Min} = O(\log n).
\]

Total amortized time:
- Inserts: \(O(n)\).
- Extract-min: \(n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n\log n)\).
- Decrease-key: at most \(m\) times, each \(O(1)\), giving \(O(m)\).

Hence,
\[
T_{\text{Fibo}}(n,m) = O(n + n\log n + m) = O(m + n\log n).
\]

---

## Comparison

Binary heap: \(O((m+n)\log n)\) (often written \(O(m\log n)\)).

Fibonacci heap: \(O(m + n\log n)\) amortized.

Thus, Fibonacci heaps asymptotically improve Dijkstra’s running time by reducing the total cost of \(\textsc{Decrease-Key}\) operations from \(O(m\log n)\) to \(O(m)\), leaving the \(n\) extract-min operations as the dominant \(O(n\log n)\) term.

student answer:
Dijkstra’s algorithm always selects the currently “closest” unsettled vertex and permanently fixes its distance, assuming no later path will be shorter. This assumption only holds when every edge weight is non-negative, because adding more edges can only increase or leave unchanged an already minimal cost. If a graph contains a negative edge, a path that reaches a settled vertex later could subtract cost and create a total distance smaller than the one Dijkstra has already locked in. Since the algorithm never revisits or relaxes a settled vertex, it cannot correct this error. Consequently, the final distances and any paths derived from them may be incorrect.
true solution:
Dijkstra’s algorithm relies on the greedy property that when it extracts (settles) a vertex \(u\) of minimum tentative distance \(d[u]\) among all unsettled vertices, this value is final, i.e.
\[
d[u]=\delta(s,u),
\]
where \(\delta(s,u)\) is the true shortest-path distance from the source \(s\) to \(u\). This property is true when all edge weights are nonnegative, and it is exactly what fails with negative edge weights.

**Why the greedy step is correct for nonnegative weights.**  
Maintain the invariant that \(d[v]\) is the length of some \(s\to v\) path (or \(+\infty\) if none has been found yet), hence \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\) always. Let \(S\) be the set of settled vertices. Suppose all edges have weight \(w(e)\ge 0\). When Dijkstra selects \(u\notin S\) with minimum \(d[u]\) and adds it to \(S\), consider any shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Let \(x\) be the first vertex on \(P\) not in \(S\), and let \(y\) be its predecessor on \(P\) (so \(y\in S\)). By relaxation when \(y\) was settled,
\[
d[x]\le d[y]+w(y,x)=\delta(s,y)+w(y,x)=\delta(s,x).
\]
Since always \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), we get \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). Because \(u\) is chosen with minimum tentative distance, \(d[u]\le d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). Also \(\delta(s,u)\ge \delta(s,x)\) along the path \(P\) when weights are nonnegative (distances cannot decrease as you extend a path), hence
\[
d[u]\le \delta(s,x)\le \delta(s,u)\le d[u],
\]
so \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). The key inequality \(\delta(s,u)\ge \delta(s,x)\) uses \(w\ge 0\).

**What goes wrong with negative edges.**  
If some edge weights are negative, a shortest path to a vertex \(u\) may go through an unsettled vertex \(v\) and then traverse a negative edge back (or forward) in a way that produces a shorter path to \(u\) *after* \(u\) has already been settled. Thus, settling \(u\) based only on current tentative distances is no longer safe: the value \(d[u]\) can still decrease later. In terms of the proof above, the step “distances cannot decrease as you extend a path” fails when negative edges exist; along a path, adding a negative edge can reduce total path length, so the “first unsettled vertex” argument no longer implies the extracted vertex has final distance.

**Concrete counterexample.**  
Let \(s\) be the source with edges:
\[
s\to u \text{ of weight } 2,\quad s\to v \text{ of weight } 5,\quad v\to u \text{ of weight } -4.
\]
The true shortest distance to \(u\) is
\[
\delta(s,u)=5+(-4)=1 \quad \text{via } s\to v\to u.
\]
Running Dijkstra:
1. Initialize \(d[s]=0\), \(d[u]=2\), \(d[v]=5\).
2. Extract-min settles \(u\) first (since \(2<5\)) and declares \(d[u]=2\) final.
3. Later \(v\) is settled; relaxing \(v\to u\) would propose \(d[u]\leftarrow 5-4=1\), which is smaller, but \(u\) has already been finalized, so the algorithm does not correct it.

Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm can return an incorrect shortest-path distance in the presence of negative edge weights because its correctness depends on the monotonicity induced by nonnegative weights; negative edges can create shorter paths to already-settled vertices.

Thus the core greedy step that underpins the algorithm’s correctness is invalid whenever negative edge weights exist.
true solution:
Dijkstra’s algorithm relies on the greedy property that when it extracts (settles) a vertex \(u\) of minimum tentative distance \(d[u]\) among all unsettled vertices, this value is final, i.e.
\[
d[u]=\delta(s,u),
\]
where \(\delta(s,u)\) is the true shortest-path distance from the source \(s\) to \(u\). This property is true when all edge weights are nonnegative, and it is exactly what fails with negative edge weights.

**Why the greedy step is correct for nonnegative weights.**  
Maintain the invariant that \(d[v]\) is the length of some \(s\to v\) path (or \(+\infty\) if none has been found yet), hence \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\) always. Let \(S\) be the set of settled vertices. Suppose all edges have weight \(w(e)\ge 0\). When Dijkstra selects \(u\notin S\) with minimum \(d[u]\) and adds it to \(S\), consider any shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Let \(x\) be the first vertex on \(P\) not in \(S\), and let \(y\) be its predecessor on \(P\) (so \(y\in S\)). By relaxation when \(y\) was settled,
\[
d[x]\le d[y]+w(y,x)=\delta(s,y)+w(y,x)=\delta(s,x).
\]
Since always \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), we get \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). Because \(u\) is chosen with minimum tentative distance, \(d[u]\le d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). Also \(\delta(s,u)\ge \delta(s,x)\) along the path \(P\) when weights are nonnegative (distances cannot decrease as you extend a path), hence
\[
d[u]\le \delta(s,x)\le \delta(s,u)\le d[u],
\]
so \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). The key inequality \(\delta(s,u)\ge \delta(s,x)\) uses \(w\ge 0\).

**What goes wrong with negative edges.**  
If some edge weights are negative, a shortest path to a vertex \(u\) may go through an unsettled vertex \(v\) and then traverse a negative edge back (or forward) in a way that produces a shorter path to \(u\) *after* \(u\) has already been settled. Thus, settling \(u\) based only on current tentative distances is no longer safe: the value \(d[u]\) can still decrease later. In terms of the proof above, the step “distances cannot decrease as you extend a path” fails when negative edges exist; along a path, adding a negative edge can reduce total path length, so the “first unsettled vertex” argument no longer implies the extracted vertex has final distance.

**Concrete counterexample.**  
Let \(s\) be the source with edges:
\[
s\to u \text{ of weight } 2,\quad s\to v \text{ of weight } 5,\quad v\to u \text{ of weight } -4.
\]
The true shortest distance to \(u\) is
\[
\delta(s,u)=5+(-4)=1 \quad \text{via } s\to v\to u.
\]
Running Dijkstra:
1. Initialize \(d[s]=0\), \(d[u]=2\), \(d[v]=5\).
2. Extract-min settles \(u\) first (since \(2<5\)) and declares \(d[u]=2\) final.
3. Later \(v\) is settled; relaxing \(v\to u\) would propose \(d[u]\leftarrow 5-4=1\), which is smaller, but \(u\) has already been finalized, so the algorithm does not correct it.

Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm can return an incorrect shortest-path distance in the presence of negative edge weights because its correctness depends on the monotonicity induced by nonnegative weights; negative edges can create shorter paths to already-settled vertices.

student answer:
Maintain the loop invariant: whenever a vertex v is removed from the priority queue and placed in the settled set S, dist[v] equals the true shortest-path distance from the source s to v, and every unsettled vertex w has dist[w] equal to the length of the shortest s-w path whose interior vertices lie entirely in S. 2) The invariant holds initially because S is empty, dist[s]=0, and all other distances are ∞. 3) Assume it holds before an iteration and let u (with minimum dist) be extracted. 4) If a shorter s-u path existed, let x be the first vertex on that path not in S and y its predecessor in S; then length(s-u) ≥ dist[y]+w(y,x). 5) When y was settled, relaxation ensured dist[x] ≤ dist[y]+w(y,x), so dist[u] ≤ dist[x] ≤ length(s-u), contradicting the supposed shorter path. 6) Thus dist[u] is optimal, preserving the invariant. 7) Subsequent relaxations may decrease dist for unsettled vertices but never alter settled ones, so the invariant continues to hold. 8) By induction the invariant is true at every removal, and when the queue is empty every vertex is settled. 9) Consequently each recorded distance equals the weight of the shortest path from s to that vertex. 10) Therefore Dijkstra’s algorithm is correct.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph with edge-weight function \(w:E\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) (nonnegative weights). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any \(v\in V\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length of a shortest \(s\to v\) path (with \(\delta(s,v)=+\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable).

Dijkstra’s algorithm maintains for each vertex \(v\) a tentative distance \(d[v]\) and a set \(S\subseteq V\) of “settled” vertices. Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(d[v]=+\infty\) for \(v\neq s\), and \(S=\varnothing\). Repeatedly, while there exists an unsettled vertex with finite tentative distance, choose an unsettled vertex \(u\notin S\) minimizing \(d[u]\), add \(u\) to \(S\), and for each outgoing edge \((u,v)\in E\) perform relaxation:
\[
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)\text{ then set } d[v]\leftarrow d[u]+w(u,v)\ (\text{and record predecessor}).
\]
We prove that upon termination, for all \(v\in V\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\); moreover, for each settled vertex \(u\), \(d[u]\) is final when \(u\) is added to \(S\).

### Lemma 1 (Upper-bound property)
At all times during the algorithm, for every vertex \(v\), \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Initially, \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\) and \(d[v]=+\infty\ge \delta(s,v)\) for \(v\neq s\). The only operation that changes a distance is relaxation along some edge \((x,v)\), setting \(d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\). By induction hypothesis, \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), so
\[
d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,x)+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,v),
\]
since a shortest path to \(v\) cannot be longer than going via \(x\). Thus the invariant holds throughout. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Settled vertices are correct)
When a vertex \(u\) is added to \(S\) (i.e., selected as the unsettled vertex with minimum \(d[\cdot]\)), then \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\).

**Proof.** Fix an iteration in which \(u\) is chosen, and let \(S\) denote the settled set just before adding \(u\). If \(\delta(s,u)=+\infty\), then \(d[u]=+\infty\) by Lemma 1 and by the fact that relaxations can only create finite values along reachable paths; the claim holds. Assume \(\delta(s,u)<\infty\).

Consider a shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Traverse \(P\) from \(s\) and let \(y\) be the first vertex on \(P\) that is not in \(S\) (such a vertex exists because \(u\notin S\) before selection). Let \(x\) be the predecessor of \(y\) on \(P\); then \(x\in S\) and \((x,y)\in E\). By choice of \(y\), all vertices before \(y\) on \(P\) are in \(S\), in particular \(x\in S\).

We claim \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is true for the first settled vertex \(s\), and more generally it follows inductively from the present lemma for earlier iterations (formally, we are proving the lemma by induction on the number of settled vertices; see below). Using \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\), when \(x\) was settled, the algorithm relaxed \((x,y)\), yielding
\[
d[y] \le d[x] + w(x,y) = \delta(s,x)+w(x,y) = \delta(s,y),
\]
where the last equality holds because \((x,y)\) lies on the shortest path \(P\). By Lemma 1, \(d[y]\ge \delta(s,y)\). Hence \(d[y]=\delta(s,y)\).

Since \(u\) is chosen to minimize \(d[\cdot]\) among vertices not in \(S\), and \(y\notin S\), we have \(d[u]\le d[y]=\delta(s,y)\). Also, along path \(P\), distances are nondecreasing because weights are nonnegative; in particular \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\). Therefore
\[
d[u] \le \delta(s,y) \le \delta(s,u).
\]
Combining with Lemma 1 (which gives \(d[u]\ge \delta(s,u)\)), we conclude \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). \(\square\)

*Induction note:* The argument above uses correctness of previously settled vertices to justify \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is valid by induction on iterations: base case is \(s\) (first selected, with \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\)); inductive step is exactly Lemma 2.

### Theorem (Correctness of Dijkstra’s algorithm)
At termination, for every vertex \(v\in V\), the algorithm outputs \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\). If predecessors are recorded on each successful relaxation, then the predecessor pointers reconstruct a shortest path from \(s\) to each reachable \(v\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 2, every time a vertex is added to \(S\), its distance becomes and remains equal to the true shortest-path distance. The algorithm continues selecting vertices until no unsettled vertex has finite \(d[\cdot]\); all reachable vertices will have been selected (their shortest-path distances are finite, and the relaxation process maintains finite tentative distances along reachable frontiers), and all unreachable vertices will retain \(d[v]=+\infty=\delta(s,v)\). Hence, for all \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) at termination.

For path reconstruction, whenever \(d[v]\) is decreased by relaxing \((u,v)\), set \(\pi[v]=u\). At the end, for any reachable \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) and the final relaxation that set \(d[v]\) certifies \(d[v]=d[\pi[v]]+w(\pi[v],v)\). Repeatedly following predecessors yields a directed path from \(s\) to \(v\) whose total weight telescopes to \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\), hence it is a shortest path. \(\square\)

The nonnegativity assumption \(w(e)\ge 0\) is essential in Lemma 2 when using \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\); without it, selecting the minimum tentative distance does not guarantee finality.

Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph with edge-weight function \(w:E\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) (nonnegative weights). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any \(v\in V\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length of a shortest \(s\to v\) path (with \(\delta(s,v)=+\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable).

Dijkstra’s algorithm maintains for each vertex \(v\) a tentative distance \(d[v]\) and a set \(S\subseteq V\) of “settled” vertices. Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(d[v]=+\infty\) for \(v\neq s\), and \(S=\varnothing\). Repeatedly, while there exists an unsettled vertex with finite tentative distance, choose an unsettled vertex \(u\notin S\) minimizing \(d[u]\), add \(u\) to \(S\), and for each outgoing edge \((u,v)\in E\) perform relaxation:
\[
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)\text{ then set } d[v]\leftarrow d[u]+w(u,v)\ (\text{and record predecessor}).
\]
We prove that upon termination, for all \(v\in V\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\); moreover, for each settled vertex \(u\), \(d[u]\) is final when \(u\) is added to \(S\).

### Lemma 1 (Upper-bound property)
At all times during the algorithm, for every vertex \(v\), \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Initially, \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\) and \(d[v]=+\infty\ge \delta(s,v)\) for \(v\neq s\). The only operation that changes a distance is relaxation along some edge \((x,v)\), setting \(d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\). By induction hypothesis, \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), so
\[
d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,x)+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,v),
\]
since a shortest path to \(v\) cannot be longer than going via \(x\). Thus the invariant holds throughout. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Settled vertices are correct)
When a vertex \(u\) is added to \(S\) (i.e., selected as the unsettled vertex with minimum \(d[\cdot]\)), then \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\).

**Proof.** Fix an iteration in which \(u\) is chosen, and let \(S\) denote the settled set just before adding \(u\). If \(\delta(s,u)=+\infty\), then \(d[u]=+\infty\) by Lemma 1 and by the fact that relaxations can only create finite values along reachable paths; the claim holds. Assume \(\delta(s,u)<\infty\).

Consider a shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Traverse \(P\) from \(s\) and let \(y\) be the first vertex on \(P\) that is not in \(S\) (such a vertex exists because \(u\notin S\) before selection). Let \(x\) be the predecessor of \(y\) on \(P\); then \(x\in S\) and \((x,y)\in E\). By choice of \(y\), all vertices before \(y\) on \(P\) are in \(S\), in particular \(x\in S\).

We claim \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is true for the first settled vertex \(s\), and more generally it follows inductively from the present lemma for earlier iterations (formally, we are proving the lemma by induction on the number of settled vertices; see below). Using \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\), when \(x\) was settled, the algorithm relaxed \((x,y)\), yielding
\[
d[y] \le d[x] + w(x,y) = \delta(s,x)+w(x,y) = \delta(s,y),
\]
where the last equality holds because \((x,y)\) lies on the shortest path \(P\). By Lemma 1, \(d[y]\ge \delta(s,y)\). Hence \(d[y]=\delta(s,y)\).

Since \(u\) is chosen to minimize \(d[\cdot]\) among vertices not in \(S\), and \(y\notin S\), we have \(d[u]\le d[y]=\delta(s,y)\). Also, along path \(P\), distances are nondecreasing because weights are nonnegative; in particular \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\). Therefore
\[
d[u] \le \delta(s,y) \le \delta(s,u).
\]
Combining with Lemma 1 (which gives \(d[u]\ge \delta(s,u)\)), we conclude \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). \(\square\)

*Induction note:* The argument above uses correctness of previously settled vertices to justify \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is valid by induction on iterations: base case is \(s\) (first selected, with \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\)); inductive step is exactly Lemma 2.

### Theorem (Correctness of Dijkstra’s algorithm)
At termination, for every vertex \(v\in V\), the algorithm outputs \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\). If predecessors are recorded on each successful relaxation, then the predecessor pointers reconstruct a shortest path from \(s\) to each reachable \(v\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 2, every time a vertex is added to \(S\), its distance becomes and remains equal to the true shortest-path distance. The algorithm continues selecting vertices until no unsettled vertex has finite \(d[\cdot]\); all reachable vertices will have been selected (their shortest-path distances are finite, and the relaxation process maintains finite tentative distances along reachable frontiers), and all unreachable vertices will retain \(d[v]=+\infty=\delta(s,v)\). Hence, for all \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) at termination.

For path reconstruction, whenever \(d[v]\) is decreased by relaxing \((u,v)\), set \(\pi[v]=u\). At the end, for any reachable \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) and the final relaxation that set \(d[v]\) certifies \(d[v]=d[\pi[v]]+w(\pi[v],v)\). Repeatedly following predecessors yields a directed path from \(s\) to \(v\) whose total weight telescopes to \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\), hence it is a shortest path. \(\square\)

The nonnegativity assumption \(w(e)\ge 0\) is essential in Lemma 2 when using \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\); without it, selecting the minimum tentative distance does not guarantee finality.
