**Proof.** Initially, \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\) and \(d[v]=+\infty\ge \delta(s,v)\) for \(v\neq s\). The only operation that changes a distance is relaxation along some edge \((x,v)\), setting \(d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\). By induction hypothesis, \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), so
\[
d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,x)+w(x,v)\ \ge\ \delta(s,v),
\]
since a shortest path to \(v\) cannot be longer than going via \(x\). Thus the invariant holds throughout. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Settled vertices are correct)
When a vertex \(u\) is added to \(S\) (i.e., selected as the unsettled vertex with minimum \(d[\cdot]\)), then \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\).

**Proof.** Fix an iteration in which \(u\) is chosen, and let \(S\) denote the settled set just before adding \(u\). If \(\delta(s,u)=+\infty\), then \(d[u]=+\infty\) by Lemma 1 and by the fact that relaxations can only create finite values along reachable paths; the claim holds. Assume \(\delta(s,u)<\infty\).

Consider a shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Traverse \(P\) from \(s\) and let \(y\) be the first vertex on \(P\) that is not in \(S\) (such a vertex exists because \(u\notin S\) before selection). Let \(x\) be the predecessor of \(y\) on \(P\); then \(x\in S\) and \((x,y)\in E\). By choice of \(y\), all vertices before \(y\) on \(P\) are in \(S\), in particular \(x\in S\).

We claim \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is true for the first settled vertex \(s\), and more generally it follows inductively from the present lemma for earlier iterations (formally, we are proving the lemma by induction on the number of settled vertices; see below). Using \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\), when \(x\) was settled, the algorithm relaxed \((x,y)\), yielding
\[
d[y] \le d[x] + w(x,y) = \delta(s,x)+w(x,y) = \delta(s,y),
\]
where the last equality holds because \((x,y)\) lies on the shortest path \(P\). By Lemma 1, \(d[y]\ge \delta(s,y)\). Hence \(d[y]=\delta(s,y)\).

Since \(u\) is chosen to minimize \(d[\cdot]\) among vertices not in \(S\), and \(y\notin S\), we have \(d[u]\le d[y]=\delta(s,y)\). Also, along path \(P\), distances are nondecreasing because weights are nonnegative; in particular \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\). Therefore
\[
d[u] \le \delta(s,y) \le \delta(s,u).
\]
Combining with Lemma 1 (which gives \(d[u]\ge \delta(s,u)\)), we conclude \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). \(\square\)

*Induction note:* The argument above uses correctness of previously settled vertices to justify \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is valid by induction on iterations: base case is \(s\) (first selected, with \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\)); inductive step is exactly Lemma 2.

### Theorem (Correctness of Dijkstra’s algorithm)
At termination, for every vertex \(v\in V\), the algorithm outputs \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\). If predecessors are recorded on each successful relaxation, then the predecessor pointers reconstruct a shortest path from \(s\) to each reachable \(v\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 2, every time a vertex is added to \(S\), its distance becomes and remains equal to the true shortest-path distance. The algorithm continues selecting vertices until no unsettled vertex has finite \(d[\cdot]\); all reachable vertices will have been selected (their shortest-path distances are finite, and the relaxation process maintains finite tentative distances along reachable frontiers), and all unreachable vertices will retain \(d[v]=+\infty=\delta(s,v)\). Hence, for all \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) at termination.

For path reconstruction, whenever \(d[v]\) is decreased by relaxing \((u,v)\), set \(\pi[v]=u\). At the end, for any reachable \(v\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) and the final relaxation that set \(d[v]\) certifies \(d[v]=d[\pi[v]]+w(\pi[v],v)\). Repeatedly following predecessors yields a directed path from \(s\) to \(v\) whose total weight telescopes to \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\), hence it is a shortest path. \(\square\)

The nonnegativity assumption \(w(e)\ge 0\) is essential in Lemma 2 when using \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\); without it, selecting the minimum tentative distance does not guarantee finality.

Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph with edge-weight function \(w:E\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) (nonnegative weights). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any \(v\in V\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length of a shortest \(s\to v\) path (with \(\delta(s,v)=+\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable).

Dijkstra’s algorithm maintains for each vertex \(v\) a tentative distance \(d[v]\) and a set \(S\subseteq V\) of “settled” vertices. Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(d[v]=+\infty\) for \(v\neq s\), and \(S=\varnothing\). Repeatedly, while there exists an unsettled vertex with finite tentative distance, choose an unsettled vertex \(u\notin S\) minimizing \(d[u]\), add \(u\) to \(S\), and for each outgoing edge \((u,v)\in E\) perform relaxation:
\[
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)\text{ then set } d[v]\leftarrow d[u]+w(u,v)\ (\text{and record predecessor}).
\]
We prove that upon termination, for all \(v\in V\), \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\); moreover, for each settled vertex \(u\), \(d[u]\) is final when \(u\) is added to \(S\).

At all times during the algorithm, for every vertex \(v\), \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Initially, \(d[s]=0=\delta(s,s)\) and \(d[v]=+\infty\ge \delta(s,v)\) for \(v\neq s\). The only operation that changes a distance is relaxation along some edge \((x,v)\), setting \(d[v]=d[x]+w(x,v)\). By induction hypothesis, \(d[x]\ge \delta(s,x)\), so
\[

\]
since a shortest path to \(v\) cannot be longer than going via \(x\). Thus the invariant holds throughout. \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Settled vertices are correct)
When a vertex \(u\) is added to \(S\) (i.e., selected as the unsettled vertex with minimum \(d[\cdot]\)), then \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\).

**Proof.** Fix an iteration in which \(u\) is chosen, and let \(S\) denote the settled set just before adding \(u\). If \(\delta(s,u)=+\infty\), then \(d[u]=+\infty\) by Lemma 1 and by the fact that relaxations can only create finite values along reachable paths; the claim holds. Assume \(\delta(s,u)<\infty\).

Consider a shortest path \(P\) from \(s\) to \(u\). Traverse \(P\) from \(s\) and let \(y\) be the first vertex on \(P\) that is not in \(S\) (such a vertex exists because \(u\notin S\) before selection). Let \(x\) be the predecessor of \(y\) on \(P\); then \(x\in S\) and \((x,y)\in E\). By choice of \(y\), all vertices before \(y\) on \(P\) are in \(S\), in particular \(x\in S\).

We claim \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\). This is true for the first settled vertex \(s\), and more generally it follows inductively from the present lemma for earlier iterations (formally, we are proving the lemma by induction on the number of settled vertices; see below). Using \(d[x]=\delta(s,x)\), when \(x\) was settled, the algorithm relaxed \((x,y)\), yielding
\[
d[y] \le d[x] + w(x,y) = \delta(s,x)+w(x,y) = \delta(s,y),
\]
where the last equality holds because \((x,y)\) lies on the shortest path \(P\). By Lemma 1, \(d[y]\ge \delta(s,y)\). Hence \(d[y]=\delta(s,y)\).

Since \(u\) is chosen to minimize \(d[\cdot]\) among vertices not in \(S\), and \(y\notin S\), we have \(d[u]\le d[y]=\delta(s,y)\). Also, along path \(P\), distances are nondecreasing because weights are nonnegative; in particular \(\delta(s,y)\le \delta(s,u)\). Therefore
\[
d[u] \le \delta(s,y) \le \delta(s,u).
\]
Combining with Lemma 1 (which gives \(d[u]\ge \delta(s,u)\)), we conclude \(d[u]=\delta(s,u)\). \(\square\)

student answer:
With an adjacency-list representation, both BFS and DFS examine every vertex and edge exactly once, giving a time complexity of O(V + E). With an adjacency-matrix representation, scanning all neighbors costs Θ(V) per vertex, resulting in O(V²). Thus their asymptotic running times are identical for the same representation; only traversal order differs. Memory use: BFS needs O(V) for its queue, while DFS uses O(V) for its explicit or recursive stack.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a graph with \(|V|=n\) vertices and \(|E|=m\) edges. Assume the standard adjacency-list representation unless stated otherwise. BFS uses a queue; DFS uses recursion or an explicit stack. Each algorithm maintains a visited/colored state so that each vertex is processed at most once.

## Breadth-First Search (BFS)

In BFS, when a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, the algorithm scans the entire adjacency list \(\text{Adj}[u]\) and considers each incident edge \((u,v)\) (directed: outgoing; undirected: both directions appear across adjacency lists). The key counting argument is:

1. Each vertex is enqueued (and dequeued) at most once, because it is marked visited upon first discovery. Thus total queue operations are \(\Theta(n)\).
2. The total number of adjacency entries scanned over the entire run is
\[
\sum_{u\in V} |\text{Adj}[u]| = 
\begin{cases}
m & \text{(directed)}\\
2m & \text{(undirected)}
\end{cases}
= \Theta(m),
\]
so the total time spent scanning edges is \(\Theta(m)\).

Therefore, with adjacency lists the total running time is
\[
T_{\text{BFS}}(n,m)=\Theta(n+m).
\]
Space usage (beyond the input graph) is \(\Theta(n)\) for the visited/parent/distance arrays and the queue; thus auxiliary space is \(\Theta(n)\).

If instead the graph is given as an adjacency matrix, scanning neighbors of a vertex takes \(\Theta(n)\), and BFS scans neighbors for each of the \(n\) vertices, giving \(\Theta(n^2)\) time (and \(\Theta(n^2)\) storage for the matrix itself).

## Depth-First Search (DFS)

In DFS, when a vertex \(u\) is first discovered, the algorithm scans \(\text{Adj}[u]\) once, recursively (or via stack) exploring any undiscovered neighbor.

The same accounting applies:

1. Each vertex is discovered and finished at most once, so total per-vertex overhead is \(\Theta(n)\).
2. Each adjacency list is scanned once, so total neighbor/edge examinations sum to \(\sum_{u\in V} |\text{Adj}[u]| = \Theta(m)\).

Hence with adjacency lists,
\[
T_{\text{DFS}}(n,m)=\Theta(n+m).
\]
Auxiliary space is \(\Theta(n)\) for visited state and the recursion stack (worst-case depth \(n\)) or an explicit stack.

With an adjacency matrix, each call scans \(\Theta(n)\) possible neighbors, across up to \(n\) vertices, so time is \(\Theta(n^2)\).

## Summary

With adjacency lists:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|)}
\]
and auxiliary space \(\Theta(|V|)\) for both.

With adjacency matrices:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2)}.
\]

With an adjacency matrix, each call scans \(\Theta(n)\) possible neighbors, across up to \(n\) vertices, so time is \(\Theta(n^2)\).

## Summary

With adjacency lists:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|)}
\]
and auxiliary space \(\Theta(|V|)\) for both.

With adjacency matrices:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2)}.
\]

Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a graph with \(|V|=n\) vertices and \(|E|=m\) edges. Assume the standard adjacency-list representation unless stated otherwise. BFS uses a queue; DFS uses recursion or an explicit stack. Each algorithm maintains a visited/colored state so that each vertex is processed at most once.

## Breadth-First Search (BFS)

In BFS, when a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, the algorithm scans the entire adjacency list \(\text{Adj}[u]\) and considers each incident edge \((u,v)\) (directed: outgoing; undirected: both directions appear across adjacency lists). The key counting argument is:

1. Each vertex is enqueued (and dequeued) at most once, because it is marked visited upon first discovery. Thus total queue operations are \(\Theta(n)\).
2. The total number of adjacency entries scanned over the entire run is
\[
\sum_{u\in V} |\text{Adj}[u]| = 
\begin{cases}
m & \text{(directed)}\\
2m & \text{(undirected)}
\end{cases}
= \Theta(m),
\]
so the total time spent scanning edges is \(\Theta(m)\).

Therefore, with adjacency lists the total running time is
\[
T_{\text{BFS}}(n,m)=\Theta(n+m).
\]
Space usage (beyond the input graph) is \(\Theta(n)\) for the visited/parent/distance arrays and the queue; thus auxiliary space is \(\Theta(n)\).

If instead the graph is given as an adjacency matrix, scanning neighbors of a vertex takes \(\Theta(n)\), and BFS scans neighbors for each of the \(n\) vertices, giving \(\Theta(n^2)\) time (and \(\Theta(n^2)\) storage for the matrix itself).

## Depth-First Search (DFS)

In DFS, when a vertex \(u\) is first discovered, the algorithm scans \(\text{Adj}[u]\) once, recursively (or via stack) exploring any undiscovered neighbor.

The same accounting applies:

1. Each vertex is discovered and finished at most once, so total per-vertex overhead is \(\Theta(n)\).
2. Each adjacency list is scanned once, so total neighbor/edge examinations sum to \(\sum_{u\in V} |\text{Adj}[u]| = \Theta(m)\).

Hence with adjacency lists,
\[
T_{\text{DFS}}(n,m)=\Theta(n+m).
\]
Auxiliary space is \(\Theta(n)\) for visited state and the recursion stack (worst-case depth \(n\)) or an explicit stack.

With an adjacency matrix, each call scans \(\Theta(n)\) possible neighbors, across up to \(n\) vertices, so time is \(\Theta(n^2)\).

## Summary

With adjacency lists:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|+|E|)}
\]
and auxiliary space \(\Theta(|V|)\) for both.

With adjacency matrices:
\[
\boxed{T_{\text{BFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2), \quad T_{\text{DFS}}=\Theta(|V|^2)}.
\]

student answer:
Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores a graph level by level, visiting all vertices at distance k from the start before moving to distance k + 1 and is usually implemented with a queue.  
Depth-First Search (DFS) follows one branch as far as possible before backtracking, using recursion or an explicit stack.  
BFS guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph and is complete for finite graphs.  
Its main cost is memory: the frontier can grow to O(|V|) in the worst case.  
Typical BFS uses include shortest-path finding in unweighted networks, computing vertex levels, discovering connected components, and optimal-move puzzle solving.  
DFS requires only O(h) memory, where h is maximum depth, but it can pursue long dead-end paths and does not ensure minimal solutions without modification.  
DFS excels at tasks like topological sorting, cycle detection, maze or puzzle generation, enumerating all solutions, and existence checks for deep paths.  
In short, pick BFS when the shallowest solution matters and memory is available; pick DFS when memory is tight or depth-oriented analysis is needed.
true solution:
Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) are graph traversal algorithms that differ in the order in which they explore vertices and, consequently, in the structural properties of the trees they produce and the problems they are naturally suited to solve. Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a graph (directed or undirected) and let \(s\in V\) be a start vertex.

**BFS (Breadth-First Search).**  
BFS explores vertices in increasing order of their distance (minimum number of edges) from \(s\). It maintains a *frontier* of discovered but not fully processed vertices in a FIFO queue. When a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, BFS inspects all outgoing edges \((u,v)\); any undiscovered neighbor \(v\) is marked discovered, assigned a parent \(u\), assigned distance \(d[v]=d[u]+1\), and enqueued.

A key invariant is: when a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, \(d[u]\) equals the length of the shortest path from \(s\) to \(u\) in an unweighted graph. Consequently, the BFS parent pointers form a shortest-path tree rooted at \(s\).

Typical use cases for BFS:
1. **Shortest paths in unweighted graphs:** compute minimum-edge paths from \(s\) to all reachable vertices (or to a target) and reconstruct paths via parent pointers.
2. **Level/Layer computation:** determine vertices at distance \(0,1,2,\dots\) from \(s\) (e.g., “degrees of separation,” minimum number of moves in puzzles with uniform move cost).
3. **Bipartite testing in undirected graphs:** 2-coloring via BFS levels; an edge connecting same-level (same parity distance) indicates non-bipartiteness.
4. **Connectivity and reachability:** determine the connected component (undirected) or reachable set (directed) from \(s\).

**DFS (Depth-First Search).**  
DFS explores by going as deep as possible along a path before backtracking. It can be implemented recursively using the call stack or iteratively using an explicit LIFO stack. Starting from \(s\), DFS visits an undiscovered neighbor whenever possible; when a vertex has no undiscovered neighbors, DFS backtracks to its parent. DFS records a depth-first forest via parent pointers, and often records *discovery times* and *finish times* for vertices.

Typical use cases for DFS:
1. **Topological sorting (DAGs):** ordering vertices by decreasing finish time yields a topological order when the graph is acyclic.
2. **Cycle detection:** in directed graphs, the presence of a back edge (to a vertex on the current recursion stack) indicates a directed cycle; in undirected graphs, encountering an already discovered neighbor other than the parent indicates a cycle.
3. **Strongly connected components:** algorithms such as Kosaraju’s and Tarjan’s rely on DFS finish times and low-link values.
4. **Articulation points and bridges (undirected graphs):** computed via DFS tree structure and low-link values.
5. **General structural analysis:** classification of edges (tree/back/forward/cross in directed graphs) via discovery/finish times.

**Complexity and practical differences.**  
With an adjacency-list representation, both BFS and DFS run in \(\Theta(|V|+|E|)\) time since each vertex is discovered once and each edge is examined \(O(1)\) times (once per endpoint in undirected graphs). BFS uses a queue and can require \(\Theta(|V|)\) space in the worst case for the queue; DFS uses a stack/recursion and can require \(\Theta(|V|)\) space in the worst case for the recursion depth or explicit stack.

The essential conceptual difference is that BFS is *distance/level-oriented* and yields shortest paths in unweighted graphs, while DFS is *path-oriented* and yields rich traversal structure (discovery/finish times, back edges) useful for analyzing global graph properties such as cycles, topological order, and connectivity structure (SCCs, bridges, articulation points).

BFS naturally lends itself to parallel expansion of layers, while DFS is inherently sequential but can be adapted for iterative deepening.  
In summary, choose BFS when shortest paths or layer-wise processing matter and memory is ample; choose DFS when depth exploration, low memory usage, or ordering tasks like topological sort are the priorities.

The essential conceptual difference is that BFS is *distance/level-oriented* and yields shortest paths in unweighted graphs, while DFS is *path-oriented* and yields rich traversal structure (discovery/finish times, back edges) useful for analyzing global graph properties such as cycles, topological order, and connectivity structure (SCCs, bridges, articulation points).

true solution:
Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) are graph traversal algorithms that differ in the order in which they explore vertices and, consequently, in the structural properties of the trees they produce and the problems they are naturally suited to solve. Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a graph (directed or undirected) and let \(s\in V\) be a start vertex.

**BFS (Breadth-First Search).**  
BFS explores vertices in increasing order of their distance (minimum number of edges) from \(s\). It maintains a *frontier* of discovered but not fully processed vertices in a FIFO queue. When a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, BFS inspects all outgoing edges \((u,v)\); any undiscovered neighbor \(v\) is marked discovered, assigned a parent \(u\), assigned distance \(d[v]=d[u]+1\), and enqueued.

A key invariant is: when a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, \(d[u]\) equals the length of the shortest path from \(s\) to \(u\) in an unweighted graph. Consequently, the BFS parent pointers form a shortest-path tree rooted at \(s\).

Typical use cases for BFS:
1. **Shortest paths in unweighted graphs:** compute minimum-edge paths from \(s\) to all reachable vertices (or to a target) and reconstruct paths via parent pointers.
2. **Level/Layer computation:** determine vertices at distance \(0,1,2,\dots\) from \(s\) (e.g., “degrees of separation,” minimum number of moves in puzzles with uniform move cost).
3. **Bipartite testing in undirected graphs:** 2-coloring via BFS levels; an edge connecting same-level (same parity distance) indicates non-bipartiteness.
4. **Connectivity and reachability:** determine the connected component (undirected) or reachable set (directed) from \(s\).

**DFS (Depth-First Search).**  
DFS explores by going as deep as possible along a path before backtracking. It can be implemented recursively using the call stack or iteratively using an explicit LIFO stack. Starting from \(s\), DFS visits an undiscovered neighbor whenever possible; when a vertex has no undiscovered neighbors, DFS backtracks to its parent. DFS records a depth-first forest via parent pointers, and often records *discovery times* and *finish times* for vertices.

Typical use cases for DFS:
1. **Topological sorting (DAGs):** ordering vertices by decreasing finish time yields a topological order when the graph is acyclic.
2. **Cycle detection:** in directed graphs, the presence of a back edge (to a vertex on the current recursion stack) indicates a directed cycle; in undirected graphs, encountering an already discovered neighbor other than the parent indicates a cycle.
3. **Strongly connected components:** algorithms such as Kosaraju’s and Tarjan’s rely on DFS finish times and low-link values.
4. **Articulation points and bridges (undirected graphs):** computed via DFS tree structure and low-link values.
5. **General structural analysis:** classification of edges (tree/back/forward/cross in directed graphs) via discovery/finish times.

**Complexity and practical differences.**  
With an adjacency-list representation, both BFS and DFS run in \(\Theta(|V|+|E|)\) time since each vertex is discovered once and each edge is examined \(O(1)\) times (once per endpoint in undirected graphs). BFS uses a queue and can require \(\Theta(|V|)\) space in the worst case for the queue; DFS uses a stack/recursion and can require \(\Theta(|V|)\) space in the worst case for the recursion depth or explicit stack.

The essential conceptual difference is that BFS is *distance/level-oriented* and yields shortest paths in unweighted graphs, while DFS is *path-oriented* and yields rich traversal structure (discovery/finish times, back edges) useful for analyzing global graph properties such as cycles, topological order, and connectivity structure (SCCs, bridges, articulation points).

student answer:
BFS starts by putting the source s in a FIFO queue with dist[s]=0; whenever a vertex u is dequeued, every undiscovered neighbor v is given dist[v]=dist[u]+1 and is enqueued. Because the queue is first-in-first-out, all vertices at distance k are dequeued (and expand their neighbors) before any vertex at distance k+1 is dequeued. Hence, by induction on k, when a vertex v is first removed from the queue its label dist[v]=k equals the length of some path found by BFS from s to v. Suppose a shorter path of length k′<k existed; let w be the predecessor of v on that path, so dist(s,w)=k′−1. Vertex w would have been dequeued before any vertex at distance ≥k′, therefore before v. When w was processed it would have discovered v and set dist[v]≤k′, contradicting dist[v]=k. Thus no shorter path exists, and dist[v] is the true shortest-path length. The predecessor pointers recorded on each discovery edge therefore reconstruct actual shortest paths, so BFS always finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an unweighted graph (directed or undirected). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any vertex \(v\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length (number of edges) of a shortest \(s\to v\) path, with \(\delta(s,v)=\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\).

Breadth-first search (BFS) maintains for each vertex \(v\) a distance label \(d[v]\) and a predecessor \(\pi[v]\). Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(\pi[s]=\text{NIL}\), and for \(v\neq s\), \(d[v]=\infty\). BFS uses a FIFO queue \(Q\): it enqueues \(s\), and while \(Q\neq\emptyset\), it dequeues a vertex \(u\) and for each edge \((u,w)\in E\), if \(w\) is undiscovered (equivalently \(d[w]=\infty\)), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\), \(\pi[w]=u\), and enqueues \(w\).

We prove: for every vertex \(v\), when BFS terminates, \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) (and thus the predecessor pointers define a shortest path tree).

### Lemma 1 (Upper bound)
For every vertex \(v\) with \(d[v]<\infty\), there exists an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Hence \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\) is impossible; rather, \(d[v]\) is an upper bound: \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\).

**Proof.** By construction, \(d[s]=0\) and the empty path has length \(0\). Whenever BFS first discovers a vertex \(w\) from \(u\), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\) and \(\pi[w]=u\). Inductively, if there is an \(s\to u\) path of length \(d[u]\), appending edge \((u,w)\) yields an \(s\to w\) path of length \(d[u]+1=d[w]\). Repeating this from \(w\) following predecessors back to \(s\) gives an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Therefore \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Queue monotonicity)
Vertices are dequeued in nondecreasing order of \(d[\cdot]\). Moreover, at any time, all vertices currently in \(Q\) have distances in \(\{k,k+1\}\), where \(k\) is the distance of the vertex at the front of the queue.

**Proof.** When a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, any newly enqueued neighbor \(w\) receives distance \(d[w]=d[u]+1\). Since the queue is FIFO, all vertices previously enqueued (and not yet dequeued) have distance \(\ge d[u]\). Newly enqueued vertices have distance exactly \(d[u]+1\) and are placed at the back. Thus no vertex with distance \(<d[u]\) can appear behind \(u\), and no vertex with distance \(>d[u]+1\) can be inserted during processing of \(u\). Inducting over dequeue operations yields the claim. \(\square\)

### Lemma 3 (No overestimation)
For every vertex \(v\) reachable from \(s\), BFS sets \(d[v]\le \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Fix a reachable vertex \(v\) and let \(L=\delta(s,v)\). Consider any shortest path
\[
s=v_0, v_1, \dots, v_L=v.
\]
We prove by induction on \(i\) that BFS eventually discovers \(v_i\) with \(d[v_i]\le i\). Base \(i=0\): \(v_0=s\) has \(d[s]=0\). Inductive step: assume \(v_i\) is discovered with \(d[v_i]\le i\). When \(v_i\) is dequeued, BFS examines edge \((v_i,v_{i+1})\). If \(v_{i+1}\) is undiscovered then it is discovered then with
\[
d[v_{i+1}]=d[v_i]+1\le i+1.
\]
If \(v_{i+1}\) was already discovered earlier, then its existing label satisfies \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\) because BFS only assigns a finite distance once, and by Lemma 2 a vertex cannot be first discovered from a vertex whose distance exceeds \(i\) before all vertices at distance \(\le i\) have been processed. In either case \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\). Thus \(d[v]=d[v_L]\le L=\delta(s,v)\). \(\square\)

### Theorem (BFS computes shortest-path distances)
For every vertex \(v\in V\), upon termination of BFS,
\[
d[v]=\delta(s,v),
\]
with \(d[v]=\infty\) iff \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\). Consequently, for any reachable \(v\neq s\), the predecessor pointers \(\pi[\cdot]\) define an \(s\to v\) path of length \(\delta(s,v)\), hence a shortest path.

student answer:
In an unweighted graph each edge costs 1.  
BFS explores vertices layer by layer: all vertices at distance 1 are dequeued before any at distance 2, those at distance 2 before any at distance 3, and so on.  
The FIFO queue guarantees this ordering because vertices are enqueued only from currently dequeued vertices’ neighbors.  
A vertex’s distance and predecessor are fixed the first time it is enqueued.  
Assume for contradiction that BFS assigns a vertex w distance d while a shorter path of length k < d exists.  
Let x be the predecessor of w on that shorter path, so x is at distance k − 1 < d − 1.  
Vertex x would have been dequeued before the vertex that set w’s distance, causing w to be enqueued (and thus labeled) with distance k, contradicting d > k.  
Therefore BFS always assigns each vertex its true minimum distance from the source, and the stored predecessors reconstruct a shortest path.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an unweighted graph (directed or undirected). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any vertex \(v\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length (number of edges) of a shortest \(s\to v\) path, with \(\delta(s,v)=\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\).

Breadth-first search (BFS) maintains for each vertex \(v\) a distance label \(d[v]\) and a predecessor \(\pi[v]\). Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(\pi[s]=\text{NIL}\), and for \(v\neq s\), \(d[v]=\infty\). BFS uses a FIFO queue \(Q\): it enqueues \(s\), and while \(Q\neq\emptyset\), it dequeues a vertex \(u\) and for each edge \((u,w)\in E\), if \(w\) is undiscovered (equivalently \(d[w]=\infty\)), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\), \(\pi[w]=u\), and enqueues \(w\).

We prove: for every vertex \(v\), when BFS terminates, \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) (and thus the predecessor pointers define a shortest path tree).

### Lemma 1 (Upper bound)
For every vertex \(v\) with \(d[v]<\infty\), there exists an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Hence \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\) is impossible; rather, \(d[v]\) is an upper bound: \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\).

**Proof.** By construction, \(d[s]=0\) and the empty path has length \(0\). Whenever BFS first discovers a vertex \(w\) from \(u\), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\) and \(\pi[w]=u\). Inductively, if there is an \(s\to u\) path of length \(d[u]\), appending edge \((u,w)\) yields an \(s\to w\) path of length \(d[u]+1=d[w]\). Repeating this from \(w\) following predecessors back to \(s\) gives an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Therefore \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Queue monotonicity)
Vertices are dequeued in nondecreasing order of \(d[\cdot]\). Moreover, at any time, all vertices currently in \(Q\) have distances in \(\{k,k+1\}\), where \(k\) is the distance of the vertex at the front of the queue.

**Proof.** When a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, any newly enqueued neighbor \(w\) receives distance \(d[w]=d[u]+1\). Since the queue is FIFO, all vertices previously enqueued (and not yet dequeued) have distance \(\ge d[u]\). Newly enqueued vertices have distance exactly \(d[u]+1\) and are placed at the back. Thus no vertex with distance \(<d[u]\) can appear behind \(u\), and no vertex with distance \(>d[u]+1\) can be inserted during processing of \(u\). Inducting over dequeue operations yields the claim. \(\square\)

### Lemma 3 (No overestimation)
For every vertex \(v\) reachable from \(s\), BFS sets \(d[v]\le \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Fix a reachable vertex \(v\) and let \(L=\delta(s,v)\). Consider any shortest path
\[
s=v_0, v_1, \dots, v_L=v.
\]
We prove by induction on \(i\) that BFS eventually discovers \(v_i\) with \(d[v_i]\le i\). Base \(i=0\): \(v_0=s\) has \(d[s]=0\). Inductive step: assume \(v_i\) is discovered with \(d[v_i]\le i\). When \(v_i\) is dequeued, BFS examines edge \((v_i,v_{i+1})\). If \(v_{i+1}\) is undiscovered then it is discovered then with
\[
d[v_{i+1}]=d[v_i]+1\le i+1.
\]
If \(v_{i+1}\) was already discovered earlier, then its existing label satisfies \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\) because BFS only assigns a finite distance once, and by Lemma 2 a vertex cannot be first discovered from a vertex whose distance exceeds \(i\) before all vertices at distance \(\le i\) have been processed. In either case \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\). Thus \(d[v]=d[v_L]\le L=\delta(s,v)\). \(\square\)

### Theorem (BFS computes shortest-path distances)
For every vertex \(v\in V\), upon termination of BFS,
\[
d[v]=\delta(s,v),
\]
with \(d[v]=\infty\) iff \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\). Consequently, for any reachable \(v\neq s\), the predecessor pointers \(\pi[\cdot]\) define an \(s\to v\) path of length \(\delta(s,v)\), hence a shortest path.

**Proof.** If \(v\) is unreachable, BFS never discovers it, so \(d[v]=\infty=\delta(s,v)\). If \(v\) is reachable, Lemma 1 gives \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\) and Lemma 3 gives \(d[v]\le \delta(s,v)\). Therefore \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\). The path obtained by following predecessors from \(v\) to \(s\) has length \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) by Lemma 1, so it is shortest. \(\square\)

Formally, let d(v) be the true shortest-path distance from s to any vertex v, and let dist(v) be the distance recorded by BFS when v is first dequeued.  
Induction on k = d(v):  
• Base k = 0: s is enqueued with dist(s)=0, so dist(s)=d(s).  
• Inductive step: assume for all vertices with d ≤ k BFS dequeues them with correct distances; take any vertex w with d(w)=k+1. Its predecessor u on a shortest path has d(u)=k, so by the inductive hypothesis u is dequeued before any vertex at layer k+1 is enqueued. When u is processed, BFS enqueues w with dist(w)=dist(u)+1=k+1, and no later relaxation can assign a smaller value because any other path to w would have to pass through a vertex at layer ≥k, yielding length ≥k+1.  
Hence dist(w)=d(w) when w is first removed from the queue, and BFS never later changes distances.  
Therefore every vertex is reached with its exact shortest-path length, and the predecessor pointers recorded by BFS reconstruct a shortest path from s to any reachable vertex.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an unweighted graph (directed or undirected). Fix a source \(s\in V\). For any vertex \(v\), let \(\delta(s,v)\) denote the length (number of edges) of a shortest \(s\to v\) path, with \(\delta(s,v)=\infty\) if \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\).

Breadth-first search (BFS) maintains for each vertex \(v\) a distance label \(d[v]\) and a predecessor \(\pi[v]\). Initially \(d[s]=0\), \(\pi[s]=\text{NIL}\), and for \(v\neq s\), \(d[v]=\infty\). BFS uses a FIFO queue \(Q\): it enqueues \(s\), and while \(Q\neq\emptyset\), it dequeues a vertex \(u\) and for each edge \((u,w)\in E\), if \(w\) is undiscovered (equivalently \(d[w]=\infty\)), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\), \(\pi[w]=u\), and enqueues \(w\).

We prove: for every vertex \(v\), when BFS terminates, \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) (and thus the predecessor pointers define a shortest path tree).

### Lemma 1 (Upper bound)
For every vertex \(v\) with \(d[v]<\infty\), there exists an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Hence \(d[v]\ge \delta(s,v)\) is impossible; rather, \(d[v]\) is an upper bound: \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\).

**Proof.** By construction, \(d[s]=0\) and the empty path has length \(0\). Whenever BFS first discovers a vertex \(w\) from \(u\), it sets \(d[w]=d[u]+1\) and \(\pi[w]=u\). Inductively, if there is an \(s\to u\) path of length \(d[u]\), appending edge \((u,w)\) yields an \(s\to w\) path of length \(d[u]+1=d[w]\). Repeating this from \(w\) following predecessors back to \(s\) gives an \(s\to v\) path of length \(d[v]\). Therefore \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Queue monotonicity)
Vertices are dequeued in nondecreasing order of \(d[\cdot]\). Moreover, at any time, all vertices currently in \(Q\) have distances in \(\{k,k+1\}\), where \(k\) is the distance of the vertex at the front of the queue.

**Proof.** When a vertex \(u\) is dequeued, any newly enqueued neighbor \(w\) receives distance \(d[w]=d[u]+1\). Since the queue is FIFO, all vertices previously enqueued (and not yet dequeued) have distance \(\ge d[u]\). Newly enqueued vertices have distance exactly \(d[u]+1\) and are placed at the back. Thus no vertex with distance \(<d[u]\) can appear behind \(u\), and no vertex with distance \(>d[u]+1\) can be inserted during processing of \(u\). Inducting over dequeue operations yields the claim. \(\square\)

### Lemma 3 (No overestimation)
For every vertex \(v\) reachable from \(s\), BFS sets \(d[v]\le \delta(s,v)\).

**Proof.** Fix a reachable vertex \(v\) and let \(L=\delta(s,v)\). Consider any shortest path
\[
s=v_0, v_1, \dots, v_L=v.
\]
We prove by induction on \(i\) that BFS eventually discovers \(v_i\) with \(d[v_i]\le i\). Base \(i=0\): \(v_0=s\) has \(d[s]=0\). Inductive step: assume \(v_i\) is discovered with \(d[v_i]\le i\). When \(v_i\) is dequeued, BFS examines edge \((v_i,v_{i+1})\). If \(v_{i+1}\) is undiscovered then it is discovered then with
\[
d[v_{i+1}]=d[v_i]+1\le i+1.
\]
If \(v_{i+1}\) was already discovered earlier, then its existing label satisfies \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\) because BFS only assigns a finite distance once, and by Lemma 2 a vertex cannot be first discovered from a vertex whose distance exceeds \(i\) before all vertices at distance \(\le i\) have been processed. In either case \(d[v_{i+1}]\le i+1\). Thus \(d[v]=d[v_L]\le L=\delta(s,v)\). \(\square\)

### Theorem (BFS computes shortest-path distances)
For every vertex \(v\in V\), upon termination of BFS,
\[
d[v]=\delta(s,v),
\]
with \(d[v]=\infty\) iff \(v\) is unreachable from \(s\). Consequently, for any reachable \(v\neq s\), the predecessor pointers \(\pi[\cdot]\) define an \(s\to v\) path of length \(\delta(s,v)\), hence a shortest path.

**Proof.** If \(v\) is unreachable, BFS never discovers it, so \(d[v]=\infty=\delta(s,v)\). If \(v\) is reachable, Lemma 1 gives \(\delta(s,v)\le d[v]\) and Lemma 3 gives \(d[v]\le \delta(s,v)\). Therefore \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\). The path obtained by following predecessors from \(v\) to \(s\) has length \(d[v]=\delta(s,v)\) by Lemma 1, so it is shortest. \(\square\)

student answer:
Topological sorting orders the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) so that for every directed edge (u, v) vertex u appears before v in the list.  
A common algorithm uses depth-first search (DFS): perform DFS from every unvisited vertex, and when a vertex finishes, push it onto a stack; popping the stack yields the topological order.  
During DFS each vertex and each outgoing edge is examined exactly once, giving Θ(V + E) time, where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges.  
Another algorithm employs Kahn’s approach with in-degree counting: repeatedly output any vertex of in-degree 0 and delete its outgoing edges, updating in-degrees.  
Using a queue to manage zero-in-degree vertices, Kahn’s algorithm likewise processes each vertex and edge once, so it also runs in Θ(V + E) time.  
Both algorithms require Θ(V) auxiliary space (for the recursion stack or explicit queue/stack and in-degree array).  
A topological order exists only if the graph is acyclic; detection of a remaining edge or a cycle during these procedures signals failure.
true solution:
A **topological sorting** of a directed graph \(G=(V,E)\) is a linear ordering of the vertices \(v_1,\dots,v_{|V|}\) such that for every directed edge \((u,v)\in E\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in the ordering. A topological ordering exists **if and only if** \(G\) is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)**; if \(G\) contains a directed cycle, no linear order can place each vertex before its successor on the cycle.

A standard algorithm to compute a topological order is **Kahn’s algorithm (in-degree method)**:

1. Compute \(\operatorname{indeg}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\).
2. Initialize a queue (or stack) \(Q\) with all vertices of in-degree \(0\).
3. Repeatedly remove a vertex \(u\) from \(Q\), append \(u\) to the output order, and for each outgoing edge \((u,w)\in E\), decrement \(\operatorname{indeg}(w)\). If \(\operatorname{indeg}(w)\) becomes \(0\), insert \(w\) into \(Q\).
4. If the output contains all vertices, it is a topological order; otherwise, the graph contains a directed cycle (some vertices never reach in-degree \(0\)).

Correctness follows from the invariant: at every step, \(Q\) contains exactly the vertices in the remaining graph with in-degree \(0\). Any vertex \(u\) extracted from \(Q\) has no incoming edges from remaining vertices, hence there is no constraint forcing some remaining vertex to precede \(u\). When \(u\) is output and removed, the only in-degrees affected are those of its out-neighbors, reflecting removal of edges \((u,w)\). Thus each edge \((u,w)\) is “accounted for” before \(w\) can be output, ensuring \(u\) precedes \(w\) in the final order. If a cycle exists, every vertex on the cycle has in-degree at least \(1\) within the remaining subgraph, so no cycle vertex can enter \(Q\), preventing completion.

**Time complexity (adjacency-list representation):**
- Computing all in-degrees takes \(\Theta(|V|+|E|)\): initialize an array of size \(|V|\) and scan all adjacency lists, incrementing once per edge.
- Each vertex is enqueued and dequeued at most once: total \(\Theta(|V|)\).
- Each directed edge \((u,w)\) is processed exactly once when \(u\) is removed, causing one decrement: total \(\Theta(|E|)\).

Therefore the overall running time is
\[
\Theta(|V|+|E|),
\]
and the extra space used is \(\Theta(|V|)\) for the in-degree array and the queue (in addition to the input graph storage).

- Computing all in-degrees takes \(\Theta(|V|+|E|)\): initialize an array of size \(|V|\) and scan all adjacency lists, incrementing once per edge.
- Each vertex is enqueued and dequeued at most once: total \(\Theta(|V|)\).
- Each directed edge \((u,w)\) is processed exactly once when \(u\) is removed, causing one decrement: total \(\Theta(|E|)\).

Therefore the overall running time is
\[
\Theta(|V|+|E|),
\]
and the extra space used is \(\Theta(|V|)\) for the in-degree array and the queue (in addition to the input graph storage).

A **topological sorting** of a directed graph \(G=(V,E)\) is a linear ordering of the vertices \(v_1,\dots,v_{|V|}\) such that for every directed edge \((u,v)\in E\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in the ordering. A topological ordering exists **if and only if** \(G\) is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)**; if \(G\) contains a directed cycle, no linear order can place each vertex before its successor on the cycle.

A standard algorithm to compute a topological order is **Kahn’s algorithm (in-degree method)**:

1. Compute \(\operatorname{indeg}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\).
2. Initialize a queue (or stack) \(Q\) with all vertices of in-degree \(0\).
3. Repeatedly remove a vertex \(u\) from \(Q\), append \(u\) to the output order, and for each outgoing edge \((u,w)\in E\), decrement \(\operatorname{indeg}(w)\). If \(\operatorname{indeg}(w)\) becomes \(0\), insert \(w\) into \(Q\).
4. If the output contains all vertices, it is a topological order; otherwise, the graph contains a directed cycle (some vertices never reach in-degree \(0\)).

Correctness follows from the invariant: at every step, \(Q\) contains exactly the vertices in the remaining graph with in-degree \(0\). Any vertex \(u\) extracted from \(Q\) has no incoming edges from remaining vertices, hence there is no constraint forcing some remaining vertex to precede \(u\). When \(u\) is output and removed, the only in-degrees affected are those of its out-neighbors, reflecting removal of edges \((u,w)\). Thus each edge \((u,w)\) is “accounted for” before \(w\) can be output, ensuring \(u\) precedes \(w\) in the final order. If a cycle exists, every vertex on the cycle has in-degree at least \(1\) within the remaining subgraph, so no cycle vertex can enter \(Q\), preventing completion.

- Computing all in-degrees takes \(\Theta(|V|+|E|)\): initialize an array of size \(|V|\) and scan all adjacency lists, incrementing once per edge.
- Each vertex is enqueued and dequeued at most once: total \(\Theta(|V|)\).
- Each directed edge \((u,w)\) is processed exactly once when \(u\) is removed, causing one decrement: total \(\Theta(|E|)\).

Therefore the overall running time is
\[
\Theta(|V|+|E|),
\]
and the extra space used is \(\Theta(|V|)\) for the in-degree array and the queue (in addition to the input graph storage).

student answer:
Let G=(V,E) be a directed graph and let L be the list produced by Kahn’s algorithm, which repeatedly removes a vertex whose current in-degree is 0, appends it to L, and deletes its outgoing edges. Termination is certain because each iteration deletes at least one vertex and |V| is finite. We show by induction that L is a topological order whenever G is acyclic. Base: the first vertex chosen has in-degree 0, so no predecessor precedes it. Induction: assume the first k vertices in L precede all of their out-neighbors; when the (k+1)-st vertex v is selected its in-degree is 0, meaning every predecessor of v has already been deleted and thus appears among the first k vertices, so every edge (u,v) has u before v in L. Hence every edge of G is oriented from an earlier to a later vertex in L, making L a valid topological sort. Conversely, if G contains a directed cycle, every vertex on that cycle always has in-degree ≥1, so no iteration can ever remove any of them and the algorithm stops with |L|<|V|, correctly signalling the absence of a topological order. Therefore the algorithm outputs a complete list exactly when the input graph is a DAG, and that list satisfies the topological-ordering property. The DFS-based algorithm is correct for the same reason: reversing vertex finishing times places each vertex before every out-neighbor discovered later, yielding an identical order. Thus topological sorting algorithms are correct.
true solution:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a directed graph. A **topological order** of \(G\) is a linear ordering \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\) of \(V\) such that for every edge \((u,v)\in E\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in the order.

We prove correctness of the standard topological-sorting algorithm (Kahn’s algorithm):

1. Compute \(\deg^{-}(v)\) (indegree) for all \(v\in V\).
2. Initialize a set/queue \(S\) with all vertices of indegree \(0\).
3. Repeatedly remove some \(u\in S\), append \(u\) to the output list \(L\), and for each outgoing edge \((u,w)\), decrement \(\deg^{-}(w)\); if \(\deg^{-}(w)\) becomes \(0\), insert \(w\) into \(S\).
4. If \(|L|=|V|\), output \(L\); otherwise report “no topological ordering” (graph has a directed cycle).

We prove: (i) if the algorithm outputs a list \(L\), then \(L\) is a topological order; (ii) the algorithm outputs a list \(L\) of all vertices iff \(G\) is a DAG.

Throughout, let \(G_t\) denote the subgraph induced by the vertices not yet output after \(t\) iterations.

### Lemma 1 (Indegree invariant)
At every iteration \(t\), for each vertex \(v\) still in \(G_t\), the maintained value \(\deg^{-}(v)\) equals the indegree of \(v\) within \(G_t\).

**Proof.** Initially, \(\deg^{-}(v)\) is the indegree in \(G_0=G\). When the algorithm outputs a vertex \(u\), it conceptually removes \(u\) and all its outgoing edges \((u,w)\) from the remaining graph. The algorithm decrements \(\deg^{-}(w)\) exactly once per such removed edge. No other edges in \(G_t\) change. Hence after updates, \(\deg^{-}(v)\) matches the indegree in the new remaining graph \(G_{t+1}\). By induction on \(t\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Existence of a zero-indegree vertex in a DAG)
If \(H\) is a finite directed acyclic graph, then \(H\) contains a vertex with indegree \(0\).

**Proof.** Suppose for contradiction that every vertex in \(H\) has indegree at least \(1\). Start from any vertex \(v_0\). Since \(\deg^{-}(v_0)\ge 1\), choose an incoming edge \((v_1,v_0)\). Similarly choose \((v_2,v_1)\), etc., producing a sequence \(v_0,v_1,v_2,\dots\). Because \(H\) is finite, some vertex repeats: \(v_i=v_j\) with \(i<j\). Then the edges \((v_{i+1},v_i),(v_{i+2},v_{i+1}),\dots,(v_j,v_{j-1})\) form a directed cycle, contradicting acyclicity. \(\square\)

### Theorem 1 (If the algorithm outputs all vertices, the order is topological)
Assume the algorithm terminates with \(|L|=|V|\). Then \(L\) is a topological order of \(G\).

**Proof.** Consider any edge \((u,v)\in E\). Let \(u=v_i\) be the time \(u\) is appended to \(L\). At that moment, \(v\) is either already output earlier or still present in the remaining graph. If \(v\) were already output earlier, then \(v\) would appear before \(u\) in \(L\), but then the edge \((u,v)\) would go from a later vertex to an earlier vertex. We show this cannot happen.

When \(v\) is output, it is taken from \(S\), so by Lemma 1 its indegree in the current remaining graph is \(0\). At that time, if \(u\) had not yet been output, then \(u\) would still be in the remaining graph and the edge \((u,v)\) would contribute \(1\) to \(v\)’s indegree, contradicting indegree \(0\). Therefore, whenever \(v\) is output, all predecessors \(u\) with edges \((u,v)\) must already have been output earlier. Equivalently, for every edge \((u,v)\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in \(L\). Hence \(L\) is topological. \(\square\)

### Theorem 2 (The algorithm outputs all vertices iff the graph is acyclic)
The algorithm outputs \(|V|\) vertices (and thus a topological order) if and only if \(G\) is a DAG.

**Proof.**

(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose the algorithm outputs all vertices. If \(G\) had a directed cycle \(C\), then initially every vertex on \(C\) has indegree at least \(1\) from within \(C\). Removing vertices not on \(C\) cannot eliminate all incoming edges among vertices of \(C\); as long as all vertices of \(C\) remain, each retains indegree at least \(1\) within the remaining graph. Thus no vertex of \(C\) can ever enter \(S\). Therefore the algorithm could not output all vertices, contradiction. Hence \(G\) is acyclic.

(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(G\) is acyclic. We show the algorithm can continue until all vertices are output. At any iteration \(t\), the remaining graph \(G_t\) is an induced subgraph of \(G\), hence also acyclic. By Lemma 2, \(G_t\) has a vertex of indegree \(0\); by Lemma 1, such a vertex is in \(S\). Therefore \(S\) is nonempty whenever vertices remain, so the algorithm outputs one vertex per iteration until none remain, yielding \(|L|=|V|\). \(\square\)

Combining Theorems 1 and 2: the algorithm returns a topological order exactly for DAGs, and any returned order is correct. \(\blacksquare\)

2. Initialize a set/queue \(S\) with all vertices of indegree \(0\).
3. Repeatedly remove some \(u\in S\), append \(u\) to the output list \(L\), and for each outgoing edge \((u,w)\), decrement \(\deg^{-}(w)\); if \(\deg^{-}(w)\) becomes \(0\), insert \(w\) into \(S\).
4. If \(|L|=|V|\), output \(L\); otherwise report “no topological ordering” (graph has a directed cycle).

We prove: (i) if the algorithm outputs a list \(L\), then \(L\) is a topological order; (ii) the algorithm outputs a list \(L\) of all vertices iff \(G\) is a DAG.

Throughout, let \(G_t\) denote the subgraph induced by the vertices not yet output after \(t\) iterations.

### Lemma 1 (Indegree invariant)
At every iteration \(t\), for each vertex \(v\) still in \(G_t\), the maintained value \(\deg^{-}(v)\) equals the indegree of \(v\) within \(G_t\).

**Proof.** Initially, \(\deg^{-}(v)\) is the indegree in \(G_0=G\). When the algorithm outputs a vertex \(u\), it conceptually removes \(u\) and all its outgoing edges \((u,w)\) from the remaining graph. The algorithm decrements \(\deg^{-}(w)\) exactly once per such removed edge. No other edges in \(G_t\) change. Hence after updates, \(\deg^{-}(v)\) matches the indegree in the new remaining graph \(G_{t+1}\). By induction on \(t\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Existence of a zero-indegree vertex in a DAG)
If \(H\) is a finite directed acyclic graph, then \(H\) contains a vertex with indegree \(0\).

**Proof.** Suppose for contradiction that every vertex in \(H\) has indegree at least \(1\). Start from any vertex \(v_0\). Since \(\deg^{-}(v_0)\ge 1\), choose an incoming edge \((v_1,v_0)\). Similarly choose \((v_2,v_1)\), etc., producing a sequence \(v_0,v_1,v_2,\dots\). Because \(H\) is finite, some vertex repeats: \(v_i=v_j\) with \(i<j\). Then the edges \((v_{i+1},v_i),(v_{i+2},v_{i+1}),\dots,(v_j,v_{j-1})\) form a directed cycle, contradicting acyclicity. \(\square\)

### Theorem 1 (If the algorithm outputs all vertices, the order is topological)
Assume the algorithm terminates with \(|L|=|V|\). Then \(L\) is a topological order of \(G\).

**Proof.** Consider any edge \((u,v)\in E\). Let \(u=v_i\) be the time \(u\) is appended to \(L\). At that moment, \(v\) is either already output earlier or still present in the remaining graph. If \(v\) were already output earlier, then \(v\) would appear before \(u\) in \(L\), but then the edge \((u,v)\) would go from a later vertex to an earlier vertex. We show this cannot happen.

When \(v\) is output, it is taken from \(S\), so by Lemma 1 its indegree in the current remaining graph is \(0\). At that time, if \(u\) had not yet been output, then \(u\) would still be in the remaining graph and the edge \((u,v)\) would contribute \(1\) to \(v\)’s indegree, contradicting indegree \(0\). Therefore, whenever \(v\) is output, all predecessors \(u\) with edges \((u,v)\) must already have been output earlier. Equivalently, for every edge \((u,v)\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in \(L\). Hence \(L\) is topological. \(\square\)

### Theorem 2 (The algorithm outputs all vertices iff the graph is acyclic)
The algorithm outputs \(|V|\) vertices (and thus a topological order) if and only if \(G\) is a DAG.

**Proof.**

(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose the algorithm outputs all vertices. If \(G\) had a directed cycle \(C\), then initially every vertex on \(C\) has indegree at least \(1\) from within \(C\). Removing vertices not on \(C\) cannot eliminate all incoming edges among vertices of \(C\); as long as all vertices of \(C\) remain, each retains indegree at least \(1\) within the remaining graph. Thus no vertex of \(C\) can ever enter \(S\). Therefore the algorithm could not output all vertices, contradiction. Hence \(G\) is acyclic.

(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(G\) is acyclic. We show the algorithm can continue until all vertices are output. At any iteration \(t\), the remaining graph \(G_t\) is an induced subgraph of \(G\), hence also acyclic. By Lemma 2, \(G_t\) has a vertex of indegree \(0\); by Lemma 1, such a vertex is in \(S\). Therefore \(S\) is nonempty whenever vertices remain, so the algorithm outputs one vertex per iteration until none remain, yielding \(|L|=|V|\). \(\square\)

Combining Theorems 1 and 2: the algorithm returns a topological order exactly for DAGs, and any returned order is correct. \(\blacksquare\)

Hence after updates, \(\deg^{-}(v)\) matches the indegree in the new remaining graph \(G_{t+1}\). By induction on \(t\). \(\square\)

### Lemma 2 (Existence of a zero-indegree vertex in a DAG)
If \(H\) is a finite directed acyclic graph, then \(H\) contains a vertex with indegree \(0\).

**Proof.** Suppose for contradiction that every vertex in \(H\) has indegree at least \(1\). Start from any vertex \(v_0\). Since \(\deg^{-}(v_0)\ge 1\), choose an incoming edge \((v_1,v_0)\). Similarly choose \((v_2,v_1)\), etc., producing a sequence \(v_0,v_1,v_2,\dots\). Because \(H\) is finite, some vertex repeats: \(v_i=v_j\) with \(i<j\). Then the edges \((v_{i+1},v_i),(v_{i+2},v_{i+1}),\dots,(v_j,v_{j-1})\) form a directed cycle, contradicting acyclicity. \(\square\)

### Theorem 1 (If the algorithm outputs all vertices, the order is topological)
Assume the algorithm terminates with \(|L|=|V|\). Then \(L\) is a topological order of \(G\).

**Proof.** Consider any edge \((u,v)\in E\). Let \(u=v_i\) be the time \(u\) is appended to \(L\). At that moment, \(v\) is either already output earlier or still present in the remaining graph. If \(v\) were already output earlier, then \(v\) would appear before \(u\) in \(L\), but then the edge \((u,v)\) would go from a later vertex to an earlier vertex. We show this cannot happen.

When \(v\) is output, it is taken from \(S\), so by Lemma 1 its indegree in the current remaining graph is \(0\). At that time, if \(u\) had not yet been output, then \(u\) would still be in the remaining graph and the edge \((u,v)\) would contribute \(1\) to \(v\)’s indegree, contradicting indegree \(0\). Therefore, whenever \(v\) is output, all predecessors \(u\) with edges \((u,v)\) must already have been output earlier. Equivalently, for every edge \((u,v)\), \(u\) appears before \(v\) in \(L\). Hence \(L\) is topological. \(\square\)

### Theorem 2 (The algorithm outputs all vertices iff the graph is acyclic)
The algorithm outputs \(|V|\) vertices (and thus a topological order) if and only if \(G\) is a DAG.

**Proof.**

(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose the algorithm outputs all vertices. If \(G\) had a directed cycle \(C\), then initially every vertex on \(C\) has indegree at least \(1\) from within \(C\). Removing vertices not on \(C\) cannot eliminate all incoming edges among vertices of \(C\); as long as all vertices of \(C\) remain, each retains indegree at least \(1\) within the remaining graph. Thus no vertex of \(C\) can ever enter \(S\). Therefore the algorithm could not output all vertices, contradiction. Hence \(G\) is acyclic.

(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(G\) is acyclic. We show the algorithm can continue until all vertices are output. At any iteration \(t\), the remaining graph \(G_t\) is an induced subgraph of \(G\), hence also acyclic. By Lemma 2, \(G_t\) has a vertex of indegree \(0\); by Lemma 1, such a vertex is in \(S\). Therefore \(S\) is nonempty whenever vertices remain, so the algorithm outputs one vertex per iteration until none remain, yielding \(|L|=|V|\). \(\square\)

Combining Theorems 1 and 2: the algorithm returns a topological order exactly for DAGs, and any returned order is correct. \(\blacksquare\)

student answer:
The union–find (disjoint-set) structure maintains a forest of trees where each tree represents a set, and the root of a tree is the representative of its set.  
Each element stores a parent pointer; a root’s parent pointer points to itself.  
Find(x) returns the root of x’s tree; with path compression, every node visited on the way to the root is reattached directly to that root, fl attening the tree and making subsequent operations faster.  
Union(x, y) merges the sets containing x and y by linking one root under the other.  
Union by rank (or size) attaches the root of the shallower (or smaller) tree beneath the deeper (or larger) one, preventing tall trees from forming.  
Combined, path compression and union by rank guarantee near-constant (inverse Ackermann) amortized time per operation.  
The structure supports three core operations: MakeSet(x) to create a singleton set, Find(x) to identify a set representative, and Union(x, y) to merge sets.  
These capabilities make union–find ideal for connectivity problems such as Kruskal’s minimum spanning tree algorithm, dynamic connectivity, and clustering.
true solution:
The union–find (disjoint-set union, DSU) data structure maintains a partition of a universe \(U\) into disjoint sets under two operations:

\[
\texttt{MAKE-SET}(x),\quad \texttt{FIND}(x),\quad \texttt{UNION}(x,y).
\]

It is typically represented as a forest of rooted trees, one tree per set, where each node stores a parent pointer; the root of each tree is the canonical representative of that set.

### Representation and invariants
For each element \(x\in U\):

- \(\texttt{parent}[x]\) is either \(x\) itself (if \(x\) is a root) or some other element.
- A set corresponds to all nodes in one rooted tree; the representative is the root \(r\) with \(\texttt{parent}[r]=r\).
- With union by rank, each root \(r\) additionally stores a nonnegative integer \(\texttt{rank}[r]\). Only ranks of roots are meaningful.

Invariant (union by rank): Along any parent pointer path from a node to the root, ranks are strictly increasing:
\[
x \neq \texttt{parent}[x] \implies \texttt{rank}[x] < \texttt{rank}[\texttt{parent}[x]].
\]
(When path compression is used, ranks may become non-tight bounds on height, but the invariant above continues to hold because parents are changed only to roots of at least as large rank.)

### Operations

#### MAKE-SET
Creates a singleton set:
\[
\texttt{parent}[x] \gets x,\qquad \texttt{rank}[x] \gets 0.
\]

#### FIND with path compression
\(\texttt{FIND}(x)\) returns the root representative of the set containing \(x\). Path compression flattens the tree by making every node on the search path point directly to the root:

\[
\texttt{FIND}(x):
\quad \text{if } \texttt{parent}[x] \neq x \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[x] \gets \texttt{FIND}(\texttt{parent}[x]);
\quad \text{return } \texttt{parent}[x].
\]

Correctness: The recursion follows parent pointers until reaching a root \(r\) with \(\texttt{parent}[r]=r\). Assigning \(\texttt{parent}[x]\gets r\) preserves set membership because \(r\) is already in the same tree as \(x\); only internal pointers are changed, not the partition.

#### UNION with union by rank
To merge the sets containing \(x\) and \(y\), first find their roots and then attach the lower-rank root under the higher-rank root. If ranks are equal, choose one as new root and increment its rank.

\[
\texttt{UNION}(x,y):
\quad r_x \gets \texttt{FIND}(x),\; r_y \gets \texttt{FIND}(y);
\quad \text{if } r_x = r_y \text{ return;}
\]
\[
\text{if } \texttt{rank}[r_x] < \texttt{rank}[r_y] \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[r_x]\gets r_y
\]
\[
\text{else if } \texttt{rank}[r_x] > \texttt{rank}[r_y] \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[r_y]\gets r_x
\]
\[
\text{else } \texttt{parent}[r_y]\gets r_x;\; \texttt{rank}[r_x]\gets \texttt{rank}[r_x]+1.
\]

Correctness: If \(r_x\neq r_y\), making one root’s parent point to the other root connects the two trees into one, hence merges the two sets and preserves disjointness of all other sets. The rank rule maintains the rank-increasing invariant on parent pointers.

### Rank meaning and height bound (union by rank alone)
A key property is that a root’s rank increases only when two roots of the same rank are unioned. By induction, any root of rank \(k\) has at least \(2^k\) nodes in its tree: initially, rank \(0\) trees have size \(1=2^0\); when rank increases from \(k\) to \(k+1\), it occurs by merging two disjoint trees each of size at least \(2^k\), yielding size at least \(2^{k+1}\). Therefore, if there are \(n\) elements,
\[
\texttt{rank} \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor,
\]
and with union by rank (without path compression) tree height is \(O(\log n)\), so operations are \(O(\log n)\) worst-case.

### Time complexity with both heuristics
With both union by rank and path compression, for any sequence of \(m\) operations on \(n\) elements (with \(m \ge n\)), the total running time is
\[
O\bigl(m\,\alpha(n)\bigr),
\]
where \(\alpha(\cdot)\) is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows so slowly that \(\alpha(n)\le 4\) for all practical input sizes. Thus each operation runs in amortized \(O(\alpha(n))\) time.

Space usage is \(O(n)\) for the parent and rank arrays.

A union–find (disjoint-set) structure maintains a collection of disjoint sets under two operations:  
1) find(x) returns the representative (root) of the set containing element x,  
2) union(x, y) merges the two sets containing x and y.

It is stored as a forest of rooted trees where each node points to its parent; the root is the set representative.  
Path compression optimizes find: while climbing from x to the root, it makes every visited node point directly to that root, flattening the tree and making subsequent finds near-constant time.  
Union by rank (or size) optimizes union: each root keeps a rank (an upper bound on tree height); when merging two roots, the root with smaller rank becomes the child of the root with larger rank, and if ranks are equal the new root’s rank increases by one.

Combined, these heuristics guarantee amortized time per operation of O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for all practical input sizes, while using only O(n) memory.
true solution:
The union–find (disjoint-set union, DSU) data structure maintains a partition of a universe \(U\) into disjoint sets under two operations:

\[
\texttt{MAKE-SET}(x),\quad \texttt{FIND}(x),\quad \texttt{UNION}(x,y).
\]

It is typically represented as a forest of rooted trees, one tree per set, where each node stores a parent pointer; the root of each tree is the canonical representative of that set.

### Representation and invariants
For each element \(x\in U\):

- \(\texttt{parent}[x]\) is either \(x\) itself (if \(x\) is a root) or some other element.
- A set corresponds to all nodes in one rooted tree; the representative is the root \(r\) with \(\texttt{parent}[r]=r\).
- With union by rank, each root \(r\) additionally stores a nonnegative integer \(\texttt{rank}[r]\). Only ranks of roots are meaningful.

Invariant (union by rank): Along any parent pointer path from a node to the root, ranks are strictly increasing:
\[
x \neq \texttt{parent}[x] \implies \texttt{rank}[x] < \texttt{rank}[\texttt{parent}[x]].
\]
(When path compression is used, ranks may become non-tight bounds on height, but the invariant above continues to hold because parents are changed only to roots of at least as large rank.)

### Operations

#### MAKE-SET
Creates a singleton set:
\[
\texttt{parent}[x] \gets x,\qquad \texttt{rank}[x] \gets 0.
\]

#### FIND with path compression
\(\texttt{FIND}(x)\) returns the root representative of the set containing \(x\). Path compression flattens the tree by making every node on the search path point directly to the root:

\[
\texttt{FIND}(x):
\quad \text{if } \texttt{parent}[x] \neq x \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[x] \gets \texttt{FIND}(\texttt{parent}[x]);
\quad \text{return } \texttt{parent}[x].
\]

Correctness: The recursion follows parent pointers until reaching a root \(r\) with \(\texttt{parent}[r]=r\). Assigning \(\texttt{parent}[x]\gets r\) preserves set membership because \(r\) is already in the same tree as \(x\); only internal pointers are changed, not the partition.

#### UNION with union by rank
To merge the sets containing \(x\) and \(y\), first find their roots and then attach the lower-rank root under the higher-rank root. If ranks are equal, choose one as new root and increment its rank.

\[
\texttt{UNION}(x,y):
\quad r_x \gets \texttt{FIND}(x),\; r_y \gets \texttt{FIND}(y);
\quad \text{if } r_x = r_y \text{ return;}
\]
\[
\text{if } \texttt{rank}[r_x] < \texttt{rank}[r_y] \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[r_x]\gets r_y
\]
\[
\text{else if } \texttt{rank}[r_x] > \texttt{rank}[r_y] \text{ then } \texttt{parent}[r_y]\gets r_x
\]
\[
\text{else } \texttt{parent}[r_y]\gets r_x;\; \texttt{rank}[r_x]\gets \texttt{rank}[r_x]+1.
\]

Correctness: If \(r_x\neq r_y\), making one root’s parent point to the other root connects the two trees into one, hence merges the two sets and preserves disjointness of all other sets. The rank rule maintains the rank-increasing invariant on parent pointers.

### Rank meaning and height bound (union by rank alone)
A key property is that a root’s rank increases only when two roots of the same rank are unioned. By induction, any root of rank \(k\) has at least \(2^k\) nodes in its tree: initially, rank \(0\) trees have size \(1=2^0\); when rank increases from \(k\) to \(k+1\), it occurs by merging two disjoint trees each of size at least \(2^k\), yielding size at least \(2^{k+1}\). Therefore, if there are \(n\) elements,
\[
\texttt{rank} \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor,
\]
and with union by rank (without path compression) tree height is \(O(\log n)\), so operations are \(O(\log n)\) worst-case.

### Time complexity with both heuristics
With both union by rank and path compression, for any sequence of \(m\) operations on \(n\) elements (with \(m \ge n\)), the total running time is
\[
O\bigl(m\,\alpha(n)\bigr),
\]
where \(\alpha(\cdot)\) is the inverse Ackermann function, which grows so slowly that \(\alpha(n)\le 4\) for all practical input sizes. Thus each operation runs in amortized \(O(\alpha(n))\) time.

Space usage is \(O(n)\) for the parent and rank arrays.

student answer:
Make-Set runs in O(1).  
If no heuristics are used, a sequence of m Finds and Unions on n elements can take Θ(m n) time, i.e., Θ(n) per operation in the worst case.  
Using union-by-size or union-by-rank alone limits tree height to ⌈log₂n⌉, giving Find and Union worst-case and amortized cost O(log n).  
Using path compression alone yields an amortized cost of O(log* n) per operation.  
Combining union-by-rank (or size) with path compression gives the best bound: a sequence of m operations on n elements runs in O(m α(n)) time, so each operation costs O(α(n)) amortized.

true solution:
Consider the disjoint-set (union–find) data structure on \(n\) elements with operations

- \(\texttt{MAKE-SET}(x)\): create singleton set \(\{x\}\),
- \(\texttt{FIND}(x)\): return a canonical representative of the set containing \(x\),
- \(\texttt{UNION}(x,y)\): replace the two sets containing \(x\) and \(y\) by their union, typically implemented as \(\texttt{LINK}(\texttt{FIND}(x),\texttt{FIND}(y))\).

We analyze amortized running time over a sequence of \(m\) operations.

Assumptions: The structure is implemented as a forest of rooted trees, where each node stores a parent pointer; a root is its own parent. A \(\texttt{FIND}\) follows parent pointers to a root. A \(\texttt{UNION}\) links one root under the other. We consider the standard heuristics:

1. Union by rank (or by size): always attach the root of smaller rank under larger rank (ties handled by increasing the winner’s rank by 1).
2. Path compression: during \(\texttt{FIND}(x)\), after finding root \(r\), set every node on the search path to point directly to \(r\).

We present the amortized bounds for common variants, and the tight combined bound.

---

## 1) Union by rank only (no path compression): \(O(\log n)\) per operation

Define \(\operatorname{rank}(v)\) as in union-by-rank. The key invariant:

**Lemma 1.** If a node \(v\) has rank \(r\), then the subtree rooted at \(v\) contains at least \(2^r\) nodes.

*Proof.* Initially, each singleton root has rank 0 and size 1 \(=2^0\). A rank increases only when two roots of equal rank \(r\) are linked; the new root gets rank \(r+1\) and its subtree size is at least \(2^r + 2^r = 2^{r+1}\). Ranks never decrease. ∎

Therefore, the maximum possible rank is at most \(\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor\). Along any parent pointer, ranks strictly increase (since a lower-rank root is attached under a higher-rank root; in case of equal ranks, one becomes parent and increases rank, so the child’s rank is strictly smaller than the parent’s new rank). Hence the height of any tree is \(O(\log n)\), so each \(\texttt{FIND}\) takes \(O(\log n)\) worst-case, and \(\texttt{UNION}\) takes \(O(\log n)\) (two finds plus constant-time link). Thus any sequence of \(m\) operations costs \(O(m \log n)\).

---

## 2) Path compression only (no union by rank): \(O(m \log n)\) total

Without rank/size heuristics, trees can become tall, but path compression dramatically shortens paths. A standard analysis (e.g., via the “doubling” argument on parent changes) yields that the total time over \(m\) operations is \(O(m \log n)\). (This is weaker than the combined bound below and not tight in general; it suffices to note that path compression alone guarantees sublinear inverse-Ackermann does not follow without union by rank.)

---

## 3) Union by rank + path compression: total \(O\big(m\,\alpha(n)\big)\), hence amortized \(O(\alpha(n))\)

Let \(m\) be the number of operations on a universe of \(n\) elements, where \(m \ge n\) typically. Then:

**Theorem (Tarjan).** For disjoint-set forests using union by rank (or size) and path compression, any sequence of \(m\) \(\texttt{MAKE-SET}\), \(\texttt{FIND}\), and \(\texttt{UNION}\) operations takes
\[
O\big(m\,\alpha(n)\big)
\]
time, where \(\alpha(\cdot)\) is the inverse Ackermann function. Consequently, the amortized time per operation is \(O(\alpha(n))\).

A concise proof outline with the necessary structural facts:

### Rank properties
With union by rank, ranks obey:

**Lemma 2.** Along any parent pointer \(v \to p(v)\), \(\operatorname{rank}(v) < \operatorname{rank}(p(v))\).

*Justification.* A node becomes a non-root only when it is linked under another root of strictly larger rank; if ranks are equal, the chosen parent’s rank increases, making it strictly larger than the child’s. Path compression changes parents to the root, whose rank is at least that of any node on the path; since ranks strictly increase along the original path, the root’s rank is strictly larger than any non-root on the path. ∎

Together with Lemma 1, \(\max \operatorname{rank} \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor\). Hence ranks take only \(O(\log n)\) distinct values.

### Bounding total pointer traversals via levels (inverse-Ackermann grouping)
The running time is proportional to the number of parent-pointer traversals performed by all \(\texttt{FIND}\) operations, plus \(O(1)\) per \(\texttt{UNION}\)/\(\texttt{MAKE-SET}\). Each \(\texttt{FIND}\) walks up parent pointers; path compression then rewires nodes on the path.

Tarjan’s analysis partitions nodes by “level” based on rank thresholds defined using a rapidly growing hierarchy; equivalently, one can define levels using the Ackermann function \(A\) and its inverse \(\alpha\). The crucial consequence of the level definition is:

**Lemma 3 (bounded level increases).** During the entire sequence, each node’s level can increase at most \(\alpha(n)\) times.

**Lemma 4 (charging traversals).** Each time a \(\texttt{FIND}\) traverses an edge \((v, p(v))\), we can charge \(O(1)\) cost either to (i) a level increase of \(v\), or (ii) a traversal of an edge where the parent has strictly larger level/rank-group, which can happen only \(O(1)\) times per node per level.

Intuitively, path compression makes a node’s parent jump to a much “higher” ancestor (in rank and hence in level), and because ranks on any path strictly increase (Lemma 2), a node cannot repeatedly traverse many edges without its parent’s rank/level increasing significantly. The Ackermann-based grouping is chosen so that “significant” increases happen only \(\alpha(n)\) times per node.

From Lemmas 3–4, total charged traversals are
\[
O\big(n\,\alpha(n)\big),
\]
and the remaining traversals (those not causing level increases) are also bounded by \(O(n\,\alpha(n))\) because there are only \(O(\alpha(n))\) levels and only \(O(1)\) such traversals per node per level. Adding the \(O(m)\) base cost of operations yields
\[
O\big(m + n\,\alpha(n)\big) = O\big(m\,\alpha(n)\big)
\]
for \(m \ge n\).

Therefore, the amortized time per operation is \(O(\alpha(n))\).

### Interpretation of \(\alpha(n)\)
The inverse Ackermann function grows extremely slowly: for all practical values of \(n\) (far beyond any conceivable machine), \(\alpha(n) \le 5\). Thus union–find with both heuristics is “almost constant time” per operation in practice, while the rigorous asymptotic amortized bound is \(O(\alpha(n))\).

---

## Final amortized bounds summary
For \(m\) operations on \(n\) elements:

- Union by rank/size only: total \(O(m \log n)\), amortized \(O(\log n)\).
- Path compression only: total \(O(m \log n)\) (standard bound).
- Union by rank/size + path compression: total \(O(m\,\alpha(n))\), amortized \(O(\alpha(n))\).
